proposals.title: Active Thermal Management via a Fixed Sheet in a Stretching Free Stream: Self-Similar Analysis of Heat and Mass Transfer Control
proposals.motivation: Precise control of heat and mass transfer from stationary surfaces in moving fluids is critical for advanced thermal management systems and industrial coating processes. Traditional analyses often focus on moving surfaces (e.g., stretching sheets) or uniform free streams, neglecting the combined effect of a fixed boundary with a prescribed non-uniform scalar condition and a controlled, stretching external flow. This gap limits optimization strategies for applications like cooling electronic substrates in flowing coolants or regulating deposition in manufacturing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tuning the stretching rate parameter of the free stream against the prescribed thermal/solutal profile on a fixed impermeable sheet provides a novel, effective mechanism for actively controlling boundary layer development and the resultant local and global Nusselt and Sherwood numbers.
proposals.method: The governing PDEs for momentum, energy, and concentration will be derived for a viscous, incompressible fluid. A suitable similarity transformation will be applied to convert them into a system of coupled, non-linear ordinary differential equations (ODEs). This system will be solved numerically using the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method combined with a shooting technique, implemented in MATLAB or a similar platform. A parametric analysis will systematically vary the free-stream stretching parameter, the Prandtl and Schmidt numbers, and the exponents defining the sheet's wall temperature/concentration profiles to evaluate their influence on transfer rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new family of self-similar solutions for a previously unstudied boundary condition configuration, extending the classical Blasius and Sakiadis flow paradigms. It will deliver comprehensive data and correlations showing how free-stream kinematics can be designed to enhance or suppress scalar transfer from a fixed, actively managed surface, offering direct design insight for engineers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the 'adversarial' configuration of a fixed sheet with an active scalar profile interacting with a stretching free stream, reversing the classic stretching sheet problem and creating a new control variable for boundary layer scalar management.

proposals.title: Coupled Effects of Anisotropic Slip and Nonlinear Thermal Radiation on High-Temperature Boundary Layer Flow and Heat Transfer over a Stretching Sheet
proposals.motivation: High-temperature processes in advanced manufacturing and aerospace applications involve boundary layer flows where surface slip and intense radiative heat transfer are critical. Pre-2026 studies often treat slip as isotropic and radiation with a linear Rosseland approximation, which fails to capture the directional fluid dynamics and the nonlinear radiative flux at extreme temperatures. A unified model is needed to understand the coupled thermo-fluid phenomena in such environments.
proposals.hypothesis: The anisotropic nature of wall slip, differing in streamwise and normal directions, will fundamentally alter the velocity and temperature profiles, and this modified thermal field will nonlinearly couple with the Rosseland radiation, creating unique heat transfer regimes unattainable with isotropic slip or linear radiation models.
proposals.method: The governing PDEs for momentum, energy, and mass transfer will be derived for a stretching sheet flow with anisotropic slip boundary conditions. The nonlinear radiation term will be incorporated into the energy equation using the Rosseland diffusion approximation. The system will be transformed using similarity variables to obtain coupled ODEs, which will be solved numerically via the bvp4c solver in MATLAB or a shooting technique with a Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg (RKF45) scheme. A parametric analysis will systematically vary the anisotropic slip parameters, radiation parameter (Rd), temperature ratio parameter, and Biot number to analyze their effects on Nusselt and Sherwood numbers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for analyzing high-temperature boundary layers with directional surface effects, yielding new correlations for heat and mass transfer rates. It will extend the applicability of nanofluid and porous media studies by providing a more accurate baseline model for flows over engineered or textured surfaces under intense thermal loading.
proposals.novelty: The bidirectional coupling between direction-dependent (anisotropic) hydrodynamic slip and a nonlinear radiative heat flux in a single model is unexplored. This synthesis moves beyond the common simplifications of isotropic slip and linearized radiation to reveal new interactive regimes.

proposals.title: Dimensionless Performance Mapping and Property-Decoupling for Alternative Heat Transfer Fluids: A Generalized Framework for Heat Exchanger Design
proposals.motivation: The search for advanced heat transfer fluids, including nanofluids and ionic liquids, often relies on case-by-case experimental studies, making generalized performance prediction difficult. This hinders the systematic design and optimization of thermal systems like compact heat exchangers. A framework to decouple and independently assess the influence of core thermophysical properties is critically needed for rational fluid selection and performance scaling.
proposals.hypothesis: The convective heat transfer and flow performance of any Newtonian fluid in a given geometry can be predicted by a universal correlation based on a core set of dimensionless numbers (e.g., Prandtl, Reynolds, Nusselt), where the effects of viscosity, thermal conductivity, and specific heat capacity are separable and quantifiable.
proposals.method: An experimental matrix will be designed using base fluids (e.g., water, ethylene glycol, oils) and their mixtures to independently vary Prandtl number over a wide range while controlling Reynolds number. A standardized test section, such as a tube-in-tube heat exchanger or a channel with a porous insert, will be used. Detailed measurements of pressure drop and heat transfer coefficient will be performed under controlled conditions. Dimensional analysis and multi-variable regression will be employed to develop generalized Nu = f(Re, Pr) correlations that explicitly decouple property effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a predictive performance map for heat exchanger designers, enabling rapid evaluation of novel fluids without extensive testing. It will establish a benchmark for comparing the intrinsic convective performance of nanofluids against their base fluids, separating property enhancement effects from potential flow structure changes. The resulting correlations will offer a fundamental tool for thermal system optimization.
proposals.novelty: Unlike most nanofluid studies that report combined property effects, this approach systematically decouples the contributions of individual thermophysical properties through a designed fluid matrix. It moves beyond specific fluid promotion to create a generalized, property-based predictive framework applicable to any Newtonian alternative.

proposals.title: Linear Stability and Bifurcation Analysis of Dual Flow Solutions in a Porous Medium with Non-Linear Rosseland Radiative Heat Transfer
proposals.motivation: In high-temperature engineering systems like solar receivers and nuclear reactors, radiative heat transfer is dominant and often modeled with a non-linear Rosseland approximation. Pre-2026 literature extensively documents the existence of dual flow and heat transfer solutions in porous media and nanofluid systems, but their stability under strongly non-linear radiation effects remains unexplored. Determining which solution branch is physically realizable is critical for reliable system design and avoiding unstable operational regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the non-linear temperature dependence of the radiative flux term (∼T^3 ∂T/∂y) will significantly alter the linear stability landscape of dual solution branches, potentially stabilizing branches that are unstable under purely conductive or linearized radiation models and shifting critical bifurcation parameters.
proposals.method: The governing equations for momentum and energy, incorporating the non-linear Rosseland approximation and a Darcy-Brinkman model for a porous medium, will be derived and solved numerically using a spectral collocation method to obtain dual base flow solutions. A linear temporal stability analysis will be performed by introducing infinitesimal perturbations, leading to a generalized eigenvalue problem. The eigenvalue spectrum will be solved using the QZ algorithm in MATLAB to determine growth rates and stability for each solution branch. Key parameters (radiation-conduction parameter Rd, porosity, modified Rayleigh number) will be varied to map stability boundaries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first predictive framework for flow selection in radiative-convective systems with dual solutions, directly applicable to the design of advanced heat exchangers and solar thermal systems. It will extend the stability analysis paradigm, well-established for purely convective flows, to the domain of strongly non-linear coupled radiation and convection.
proposals.novelty: While dual solutions and stability analysis are known, and non-linear radiation models are used, their intersection—specifically how the non-linear radiative term governs the stability and bifurcation of dual flows—is a novel and unexplored synthesis of pre-2026 research themes in heat transfer and fluid mechanics.

proposals.title: Non-Linear Radiative Heat Transfer and Flow Analysis of Nanofluid Film Coating on a High-Speed Stretching Sheet with Thermal Stratification
proposals.motivation: High-speed industrial film coating processes, such as in polymer or photovoltaic manufacturing, require precise thermal management during drying/curing to prevent defects like warping or uneven curing. The non-linear radiative heat flux, dominant at high temperatures, is often simplified in existing models, creating a gap between theory and scalable manufacturing realities where radiative heaters or coolers are used. A comprehensive simulation framework integrating this physics is urgently needed for virtual process optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a non-linear Rosseland radiative approximation (with T^4 dependence) into the boundary layer flow and heat transfer model for a nanofluid-coated stretching sheet will accurately predict critical thermal gradients and stresses, revealing optimal process parameters that linear models cannot identify.
proposals.method: The model extends the classical Sakiadis flow for a stretching sheet to include a nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-deionized water), thermal/solutal stratification, and a non-linear Rosseland term. Governing PDEs for momentum, energy, and concentration will be derived and transformed into ODEs using similarity transformations. The resulting highly non-linear system will be solved numerically using a shooting method coupled with the fourth-order Runge-Kutta scheme and a Newton-Raphson iterator, with sensitivity analysis on key parameters (sheet velocity, radiation parameter, nanoparticle volume fraction, stratification parameters).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-of-its-kind predictive tool for industrial coating line design, directly linking fundamental non-linear radiative heat transfer to process control. It will establish definitive correlations between radiative management, nanofluid properties, and final film quality, offering concrete optimization strategies to prevent thermal defects in large-area, high-speed manufacturing.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a full non-linear Rosseland model within a stratified, nanofluid-based coating flow on a stretching sheet addresses a critical simplification in prior industrial simulations, creating a direct bridge to practical radiative heating/cooling systems not previously modeled with this fidelity.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Slip and Transpiration Parameters in Nanofluid Flow over a Wedge Using Remote Thermal Imaging
proposals.motivation: Advanced functional surfaces, such as superhydrophobic or porous coatings, are critical for enhancing heat transfer and drag reduction in micro/nano-fluidic systems and compact heat exchangers. Direct measurement of their key interfacial parameters—slip length, thermal jump, and transpiration rate—is often intrusive and impractical. A non-intrusive, inverse methodology based on remote thermal sensing would enable the characterization of these surfaces in operational conditions, addressing a significant gap in experimental diagnostics for nanofluid and porous media flows.
proposals.hypothesis: The coupled effects of velocity slip, thermal jump, and wall transpiration on convective heat transfer produce a unique, measurable thermal signature in the downstream flow field, enabling the simultaneous inverse estimation of all three boundary condition parameters from temperature profile data.
proposals.method: A forward model will be developed by extending the Heimanz flow formulation for a wedge, incorporating velocity slip (Navier condition), thermal jump, and uniform transpiration, with nanofluid properties modeled using pre-2026 correlations (e.g., Tiwari-Das model). The inverse problem will be solved using the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm, a robust gradient-based optimization method, to minimize the difference between simulated and measured temperature profiles. Sensitivity analysis will be performed to identify optimal sensor placement, and the method's robustness will be tested against simulated measurement noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, non-intrusive diagnostic tool for characterizing functional surfaces in convective flows, directly applicable to systems using nanofluids and porous media. It will provide a new inverse methodology that links remote thermal measurements to multiple interfacial physical parameters, advancing the analysis of complex boundary conditions in heat transfer research.
proposals.novelty: While inverse heat transfer problems exist, the simultaneous estimation of slip, thermal jump, and transpiration from a single thermal field in a convective nanofluid flow is unprecedented. This approach uniquely fuses concepts from slip-flow theory, transpiration cooling, and inverse parameter estimation for surface diagnostics.

proposals.title: Inverted Mass Transfer Control in a Cone-Disk System: Analysis of Flow and Thermal Characteristics with Injection at the Disk and Suction at the Cone
proposals.motivation: In rotating cone-disk systems, the conventional use of suction at the disk and injection at the cone is a standard technique for boundary layer control and heat transfer enhancement. However, inverting this condition remains unexplored and could reveal unique flow dynamics and thermal performance regimes. Investigating this inversion is crucial for optimizing novel rotating machinery, advanced heat exchangers, and chemical vapor deposition processes where mass flux directionality is a critical design parameter.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inverting the suction/injection condition will fundamentally alter the radial and azimuthal velocity profiles, pressure distribution, and thermal boundary layer, potentially leading to enhanced local heat transfer rates and distinct vortex structures compared to the conventional configuration.
proposals.method: We will employ a numerical simulation approach using the finite volume method to solve the coupled, steady-state Navier-Stokes and energy equations for an incompressible, laminar flow. The geometry consists of a stationary cone and a rotating disk with uniform fluid injection at the disk surface and suction at the cone surface. The governing equations will be discretized using the SIMPLE algorithm, and the effects of key parameters (injection/suction strength, cone angle, rotational Reynolds number) on the Nusselt number, skin friction, and flow structure will be systematically analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset and correlations for heat and fluid flow in a cone-disk system with inverted mass transfer control. It will contribute new design guidelines for thermal engineers seeking to manipulate boundary layers in rotating systems and expand the fundamental understanding of how mass flux directionality influences convective transport in such geometries.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate inversion of the canonical suction/injection setup for cone-disk systems, a non-obvious perturbation that has not been systematically studied despite its potential to unlock different heat transfer and flow stability characteristics.

proposals.title: Thermal and Solutal Analysis of a Non-Newtonian Nanofluid Flow in a Porous Wedge with Bioconvection: A Casson-Carreau Hybrid Model
proposals.motivation: Prior research on nanofluid heat transfer often assumes Newtonian base fluids, which is inadequate for many industrial and biological applications involving polymers, slurries, or blood. Integrating realistic non-Newtonian rheology (like Casson or Carreau models) into stratified flows over permeable surfaces addresses a significant gap, with direct relevance to enhanced oil recovery, polymer processing, and biomedical device design. This study builds directly on recent work by Chamkha et al. on stratified flows and bioconvection, extending it to more complex fluid behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating shear-thinning (Carreau) and yield-stress (Casson) non-Newtonian behaviors into the base fluid will significantly alter the velocity, temperature, and concentration boundary layer profiles, leading to quantifiable enhancements or reductions in local Nusselt and Sherwood numbers compared to Newtonian nanofluids under similar bioconvection and stratification conditions.
proposals.method: The governing PDEs for momentum, energy, and mass transfer will be derived for a Casson-Carreau hybrid nanofluid flow over a porous wedge, incorporating thermal/solutal stratification, bioconvection of gyrotactic microorganisms, and chemical reaction. The model will be non-dimensionalized and solved numerically using the robust bvp4c solver in MATLAB, validated against published Newtonian nanofluid results. A parametric analysis will systematically vary the Casson parameter, Carreau power-law index, stratification parameters, and bioconvection Rayleigh number to evaluate their impact on heat and mass transfer rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the coupled effects of non-Newtonian rheology, bioconvection, and stratification in wedge flows, yielding new correlations for engineering design. It will extend the applicability of nanofluid research to a broader class of complex fluids, offering insights for optimizing thermal management systems and microbial fuel cells where rheology is critical.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of a hybrid non-Newtonian (Casson-Carreau) fluid model with bioconvection and stratification in a porous wedge geometry, a combination not previously explored in the pre-2026 literature, moving beyond the Newtonian or single rheological model assumptions prevalent in earlier nanofluid studies.

proposals.title: Thermal and Flow Analysis of Hybrid Nanofluid Flow in a Microfluidic Rotating Disk System with Porous Media and Chemical Reaction
proposals.motivation: Microfluidic rotating disk systems are critical for lab-on-a-chip applications, including chemical synthesis, biological assays, and microscale heat exchangers. However, the combined effects of rotation, microscale confinement, and complex fluid properties on heat transfer are not fully understood, limiting device optimization. This study addresses this gap by integrating advanced nanofluids and porous structures to enhance thermal performance in a compact, high-efficiency system.
proposals.hypothesis: The integration of a metallic-nanomaterial hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-deionized water) with a strategically designed porous layer on a microscale rotating disk will significantly enhance convective heat transfer and mixing efficiency compared to a plain disk or single-phase flow. The chemical reaction at the surface will further modulate the mass transfer, coupling with the thermal field.
proposals.method: A three-dimensional computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model will be developed using the finite volume method. The flow of a hybrid nanofluid (AA7072 nanoparticles in deionized water) will be simulated in a microfluidic disk geometry, incorporating a fractal interfacial layer model for the nanofluid and a Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer model for an annular porous layer. Governing equations for momentum, energy, and species concentration (with a first-order chemical reaction) will be solved under steady-state conditions. Key parameters to be varied include rotational Reynolds number, nanoparticle volume fraction, porosity and permeability of the porous layer, and chemical reaction rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide novel correlations for Nusselt and Sherwood numbers specific to hybrid nanofluid flow in porous micro-rotating disks, directly applicable to the design of next-generation microfluidic reactors and thermal management devices. It will elucidate the coupled mechanisms of centrifugal force, nanoparticle-enhanced conductivity, porous media dispersion, and reactive mass transfer on microscale heat transfer performance.
proposals.novelty: While rotating disk flows and nanofluids have been studied separately, this proposal uniquely combines them at the microfluidic scale with the concurrent integration of a porous media layer and surface chemical reaction, a configuration not extensively analyzed in pre-2026 literature. The application of a fractal model for the nanofluid interfacial layer in this specific rotating geometry is also novel.

proposals.title: Thermal and Hydrodynamic Analysis of a Hybrid Nanofluid Flow Induced by a Rotating Fluid over a Stationary Cone and Disk
proposals.motivation: The flow induced by a rotating fluid over stationary geometries, such as cones and disks, is a fundamental problem in fluid mechanics with applications in rotating machinery, chemical vapor deposition reactors, and thermal management systems. Prior to 2026, studies on rotating flows often considered single geometries or Newtonian fluids, leaving a gap in understanding the coupled thermal-hydraulic performance in complex, dual-geometry systems with advanced working fluids like hybrid nanofluids. This work addresses that gap by integrating the dual-geometry problem with contemporary heat transfer enhancement techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the presence of a stationary cone beneath a stationary disk, with a rotating fluid above, will create unique, interacting vortex structures and boundary layers that can be optimized with hybrid nanofluids to significantly enhance heat transfer rates compared to single-geometry or pure-fluid configurations.
proposals.method: The study will employ a numerical simulation approach using the finite volume method (FVM) to solve the coupled continuity, momentum (Navier-Stokes), and energy equations. The geometry will consist of a stationary disk with a coaxial stationary cone beneath it, immersed in a rotating fluid. A hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072 and Al2O3 nanoparticles in a deionized water base) will be modeled using a single-phase approach with temperature-dependent thermophysical properties. Key parameters to be varied include the cone apex angle, rotational Reynolds number, nanoparticle volume fraction, and thermal boundary conditions. The SIMPLE algorithm will be used for pressure-velocity coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide detailed flow field visualizations and quantitative data on Nusselt number and skin friction coefficients for this novel dual-geometry configuration. It will establish correlations between geometric parameters, nanofluid composition, and thermal performance, offering design guidelines for engineers. The results will extend the understanding of rotating flows beyond classical von Kármán or Bödewadt flows to more complex, industrially relevant scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous consideration of a stationary cone-disk system under a rotating fluid, a configuration not extensively studied pre-2026, combined with the application of a bioconvection-influenced hybrid nanofluid (AA7072-deionized water) for active heat transfer control in this unique flow regime.

proposals.title: Transient Double-Diffusive Natural Convection in a Porous Enclosure with Hybrid Nanofluids and Bioconvection: A Numerical Study
proposals.motivation: Most analyses of buoyancy-driven flows in porous media, especially with nanofluids and biological particles, assume steady-state conditions, which fails to capture the critical start-up and evolution phases of heat and mass transfer. In engineering applications like solar thermal storage, pollutant dispersion in saturated soils, and bioreactor design, understanding the transient dynamics of coupled thermal, solutal, and biological fields is essential for optimizing performance and control strategies. This study addresses this gap by focusing on the time-dependent behavior of these complex systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transient evolution of the Nusselt and Sherwood numbers in a double-diffusive porous system is significantly influenced by the unsteady interaction between the thermal and solutal buoyancy forces, the dynamic aggregation of hybrid nanoparticles, and the time-dependent motility of gyrotactic microorganisms, leading to distinct flow regimes not observable in steady-state analysis.
proposals.method: The governing unsteady, nonlinear partial differential equations for mass, momentum, energy, solute concentration, and microorganism density will be formulated for a two-dimensional porous enclosure. The Tiwari-Das nanofluid model will be employed for AA7072-TiO2/deionized water hybrid nanofluids, incorporating temperature-dependent thermophysical properties and a fractal interfacial layer model. The bioconvection of gyrotactic microorganisms will be coupled with solutal stratification. The dimensionless equations will be solved using the Finite Volume Method with the SIMPLE algorithm for pressure-velocity coupling. A fully implicit time-marching scheme will be used to capture the transient dynamics from initial isothermal conditions to a statistically steady state. Key parameters (Rayleigh-Darcy number, buoyancy ratio, Lewis number, bioconvection Péclet and Rayleigh numbers, nanoparticle volume fraction, and porosity) will be varied to analyze their impact on the temporal evolution of heat and mass transfer rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive map of the transient response of a coupled thermal-solutal-bioconvective system in a porous medium, quantifying the time scales to reach steady state under various governing parameters. It will deliver novel correlations for time-dependent Nusselt and Sherwood numbers, offering practical design insights for transient-dominated processes. The results will extend the foundational steady-state work of Chamkha and others into the critical temporal domain.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the first combined transient analysis of double-diffusive natural convection, hybrid nanofluids with a fractal interfacial model, and bioconvection within a porous medium, capturing the complex, time-evolving interactions between these four distinct transport mechanisms.

proposals.title: MHD Effects on Double-Diffusive Convection of a Hybrid Nanofluid in a Porous Enclosure with Thermal and Solutal Stratification
proposals.motivation: Double-diffusive convection in porous media, driven by simultaneous temperature and concentration gradients, is critical for applications like solar pond stability, contaminant transport, and advanced heat exchangers. The integration of magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) offers active control over flow and heat/mass transfer rates, while the use of hybrid nanofluids (e.g., AA7072-deionized water) promises significant thermal enhancement. A systematic study combining these pre-2026 research threads is needed to optimize such systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a transverse magnetic field will suppress convective vortices and alter the boundary layer structure in a stratified, porous medium, thereby providing a tunable mechanism to control the rates of heat and mass transfer for a hybrid nanofluid. The magnetic field's damping effect will interact non-linearly with the buoyancy forces from thermal and solutal stratification.
proposals.method: The study will employ a numerical finite-volume method to solve the coupled, non-linear governing equations for mass, momentum (incorporating Darcy-Brinkman and Lorentz force terms), energy, and species concentration. The geometry will be a two-dimensional porous enclosure with fractal interfacial layer modeling for nanoparticle interaction. Key parameters varied will be Hartmann number (magnetic field strength), thermal and solutal stratification parameters, nanoparticle volume fraction, and Darcy number. Validation will be performed against established benchmarks for MHD natural convection and double-diffusive flows.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide comprehensive parametric maps showing how magnetic fields can be used to tailor Nusselt and Sherwood numbers in stratified, double-diffusive systems. It will deliver new correlations for heat and mass transfer rates as functions of Hartmann number, stratification strength, and nanoparticle concentration, directly useful for the design of controlled thermal and chemical processing systems.
proposals.novelty: While MHD and double-diffusion have been studied separately, the integration of a transverse magnetic field with the specific complexities of thermal/solutal stratified flow of a hybrid nanofluid in a porous medium, analyzed through a fractal interfacial model, constitutes a novel and non-trivial synthesis of Dr. Chamkha's recent research themes.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Transient Boundary Heat Flux in a Porous Medium with Hybrid Nanofluids Using Interior Temperature and Concentration Data
proposals.motivation: Accurate determination of surface heat flux is critical for the design and safety of thermal systems like heat exchangers and nuclear reactors, but direct measurement is often impossible. Inverse methods using interior sensor data offer a solution, but existing pre-2026 studies rarely couple this with complex, realistic transport phenomena involving nanofluids and porous media, which are prevalent in advanced thermal applications. This gap limits the practical application of inverse techniques to modern heat transfer enhancement strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an inverse algorithm based on the Conjugate Gradient Method (CGM) with an adjoint formulation can accurately and stably reconstruct an unknown, time-dependent boundary heat flux in a porous enclosure filled with a hybrid nanofluid, using sparse interior measurements of both temperature and solute concentration.
proposals.method: The forward model will govern the transient, laminar flow of a water-based hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072 and metallic nanoparticles) in a porous medium, incorporating thermal and solutal stratification, bioconvection, and chemical reaction effects, modeled via similarity transformations. The inverse solver will employ the CGM, which minimizes a functional representing the error between computed and measured interior data, with the sensitivity problem solved efficiently using an adjoint formulation. The regularization inherent in the CGM iteration process will stabilize the solution against measurement noise. Key parameters like nanoparticle volume fraction, porosity, and stratification will be analyzed for their impact on reconstruction accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust computational framework for non-intrusive thermal diagnostics in systems utilizing nanofluid-porous media combinations. It will yield new insights into how coupled heat and mass transfer parameters influence the ill-posedness and solvability of the inverse heat flux problem. The results will directly inform the design and monitoring of enhanced heat exchangers and energy systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous inversion for boundary heat flux using dual-field (temperature and concentration) interior data within a complex transport model that integrates hybrid nanofluids, porous media, stratification, and bioconvection—a combination not addressed in pre-2026 inverse heat transfer literature.

proposals.title: Forced Convection Cooling with Nano-Encapsulated Phase Change Material (NEPCM) Slurry Past a Truncated Cone: A Numerical Study of Heat Transfer Enhancement
proposals.motivation: Active cooling of high-power electronic components and aerodynamic surfaces often involves forced convection over bluff bodies. Traditional coolants like water or air have limited thermal capacity. Nano-encapsulated phase change material (NEPCM) slurries, which leverage latent heat absorption during phase change, offer a promising pathway for significant heat transfer enhancement, but their flow and thermal behavior around complex geometries like truncated cones remain poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the forced flow of an NEPCM slurry past a truncated cone will yield a substantial enhancement in the average Nusselt number compared to a pure fluid, primarily due to the effective utilization of latent heat during the phase change process within the thermal boundary layer.
proposals.method: The study will employ a finite volume method to solve the coupled continuity, momentum, and energy equations for steady, laminar, incompressible flow. The NEPCM slurry will be modeled using the single-phase homogeneous model with temperature-dependent effective properties, including an effective specific heat capacity to account for phase change enthalpy. The geometry will be a truncated cone with varying apex angles and truncation ratios. Key parameters to be analyzed include Reynolds number, NEPCM volume fraction, Stefan number (governing latent heat effect), and cone geometry. Validation will be performed against established data for pure fluid flow over similar shapes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed parametric analysis of heat transfer and flow characteristics for NEPCM slurries in a novel truncated cone geometry. It will yield practical correlations for the average Nusselt number as a function of the governing parameters, directly aiding thermal engineers in designing compact and efficient cooling systems. The study will also elucidate the interplay between geometric factors and the phase-change-enhanced thermal boundary layer.
proposals.novelty: While NEPCM flow in channels and over simple shapes has been studied, its application to the aerodynamically relevant and thermally challenging truncated cone geometry is novel. The integration of phase-change material dynamics with the complex flow separation and reattachment patterns inherent to this bluff body represents a significant advancement beyond previous studies on simple geometries or passive porous inserts.

proposals.title: Numerical Investigation of Conjugate Heat Transfer and Phase Change Enhancement in a Wavy Enclosure with Conductive Fins and Hybrid Nanofluids
proposals.motivation: Enhancing the rate of phase change in latent heat thermal energy storage (LHTES) systems is critical for improving the efficiency of solar thermal and waste heat recovery applications. While wavy-walled enclosures and nanoparticle dispersions are known to improve heat transfer, the synergistic effect of integrating solid conductive fins within such complex geometries remains underexplored, particularly for conjugate solid-liquid phase change problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically embedding high-conductivity solid fins within a wavy phase-change material (PCM) domain will significantly enhance the effective thermal conductivity and disrupt natural convection patterns, leading to a substantially faster phase change rate compared to wavy domains or nanoparticle enhancement alone.
proposals.method: The study will employ a finite volume method to solve the transient, two-dimensional governing equations for fluid flow, heat transfer, and solidification/melting, using an enthalpy-porosity technique. The conjugate heat transfer at the fin-PCM interface will be rigorously modeled. The wavy domain geometry and fin parameters (number, length, thickness, conductivity, and placement) will be optimized using a parametric analysis. The base fluid will be enhanced with AA7072 and metallic nanoparticles, building on the researcher's prior work, to create a hybrid nanofluid PCM.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive parametric map detailing the optimal fin configuration for maximizing phase change rate in wavy LHTES units. It will establish a novel, combined enhancement strategy (fins + wavy walls + hybrid nanofluids) and quantify the relative contribution of conduction enhancement versus convection modification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conjugate analysis of solid fins within a dynamically evolving wavy PCM domain filled with chemically reactive hybrid nanofluids, a configuration not systematically studied in pre-2026 literature, which typically examines fins in simple geometries or nanofluids without conjugate solid structures.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Optimization of Heat Transfer in a Porous Enclosure with Perforated Obstacles and Hybrid Nanofluids
proposals.motivation: Enhancing heat transfer in enclosures is critical for improving the efficiency of thermal systems like heat exchangers and electronic cooling. While the individual effects of porous media, perforated obstacles, and nanofluids have been studied, their synergistic interactions and the optimal trade-offs between key parameters remain poorly quantified. A systematic investigation is needed to guide the design of next-generation thermal management systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a non-linear, synergistic optimum exists for the combined parameters of obstacle size/perforation, solid-to-fluid conductivity ratio of the porous matrix, and nanoparticle volume fraction, which maximizes the overall Nusselt number beyond the enhancement achievable by optimizing each parameter independently.
proposals.method: A numerical study will be conducted using the finite volume method to solve the coupled momentum and energy equations for laminar, steady-state flow in a differentially heated square cavity filled with a porous medium and a water-based hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-deionized water). A central, heat-conducting perforated block will be modeled. A parametric sweep will be performed for block size, conductivity ratio (1-1000), and nanoparticle fraction (0-4%). Response Surface Methodology (RSM) and a genetic algorithm (NSGA-II) will be employed for multi-objective optimization, treating the average Nusselt number and the global thermal conductance as the primary performance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive map of thermal performance as a function of three critical design variables, identifying Pareto-optimal fronts for practical design. It will deliver specific, quantitative guidelines for configuring porous media-nanofluid systems with embedded conductive obstacles, directly applicable to compact heat exchanger design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the concurrent optimization of three distinct enhancement mechanisms—conductive obstacles, porous media, and nanofluids—using a combined RSM and genetic algorithm approach, which has not been extensively applied to this specific multi-physics problem in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Adjoint-Based Multi-Objective Optimization of Porous Heat Exchanger Geometries Infused with Hybrid Nanofluids
proposals.motivation: Designing high-performance thermal systems, such as compact heat exchangers with porous inserts and nanofluids, requires optimizing multiple conflicting parameters (e.g., pressure drop vs. Nusselt number). Traditional parametric sweeps are computationally prohibitive for such complex, coupled heat and fluid flow problems. An efficient, gradient-based optimization framework is needed to navigate this high-dimensional design space for real-world engineering applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the continuous adjoint method, coupled with a RANS-based solver for non-Newtonian nanofluids, can efficiently identify Pareto-optimal designs for porous heat exchanger configurations, significantly outperforming heuristic optimization in computational cost and solution quality.
proposals.method: The conjugate heat transfer and flow of a water-based hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-Al2O3) through a channel with a porous, perforated obstacle will be modeled using the finite volume method in OpenFOAM (v8 or earlier). The continuous adjoint equations for the system will be derived and solved to compute the sensitivity of a combined objective function (maximizing heat transfer, minimizing pumping power) with respect to geometric design variables describing the obstacle's porosity, perforation pattern, and shape. The gradient information will drive a gradient-based optimization algorithm (e.g., MMA) within a discrete adjoint optimization loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust computational framework for the systematic design of enhanced heat transfer surfaces using nanofluids. It will provide new insights into the optimal trade-offs between thermal and hydraulic performance in porous media systems. The resulting Pareto frontiers and optimized geometries will serve as a benchmark for future design studies in thermal engineering.
proposals.novelty: While adjoint methods have been applied in aerodynamics, their integration with complex nanofluid models (including viscosity and thermal conductivity variations) and porous media for thermal system optimization is novel. This approach moves beyond the common analysis of fixed geometries to the automated, gradient-driven design of the porous structure itself.

proposals.title: Thermohydraulic Analysis of a Moving Array of Needles in a Nanofluid: Nanolayer and Porous Media Effects
proposals.motivation: Enhancing heat transfer in compact systems like microchannel heat exchangers is critical for advanced thermal management in electronics and energy systems. While stationary obstacles and nanofluids have been studied, the dynamic interaction of moving, needle-like structures with nanofluids—where a nanolayer at the particle-fluid interface alters effective properties—remains unexplored. This gap limits the optimization of active, high-surface-area cooling systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a strategically arranged, moving array of needles will induce synergistic flow disruption and boundary layer renewal, and that incorporating the nanolayer effect in the nanofluid model will significantly improve the prediction and enhancement of the system's overall heat transfer rate compared to stationary arrays or base fluids.
proposals.method: A numerical study will be conducted using the finite volume method (ANSYS Fluent or a custom solver) to solve the coupled continuity, momentum (Navier-Stokes), and energy equations. The nanofluid will be modeled using a two-phase mixture approach with temperature-dependent properties, explicitly incorporating a fractal interfacial nanolayer model to modify effective thermal conductivity and viscosity. The needles will be modeled as moving, isothermal walls within a porous matrix, and their arrangement (staggered/in-line), speed, and spacing will be key variable parameters analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide novel insights into the coupled thermohydraulic mechanisms of moving boundary-enhanced transfer in nanofluids. It will yield quantitative design guidelines (e.g., optimal needle spacing-to-speed ratios) for advanced heat exchangers and contribute a more physically accurate simulation framework for nanofluids by integrating dynamic boundaries with nanolayer phenomena.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the confluence of three pre-2026 themes: the dynamic analysis of a moving needle array (extending static perforated obstacle studies), the application of a fractal nanolayer model to this complex flow, and the consideration of the system within a porous media context for compact heat exchanger design.

proposals.title: Thermal Performance Analysis of a Porous Heat Exchanger with Temperature-Dependent Nanofluid Properties: A Numerical and Parametric Study
proposals.motivation: The assumption of constant thermophysical properties is a common simplification in heat transfer analysis, particularly for nanofluids in porous media systems. However, pre-2026 research indicates that properties like viscosity and thermal conductivity are strongly temperature-dependent, especially for complex fluids involving nanoparticles and chemical reactions. This assumption may lead to significant inaccuracies in predicting heat exchanger efficiency and flow dynamics, limiting the optimization of thermal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating temperature-dependent viscosity and thermal conductivity models for nanofluids will reveal significant deviations in Nusselt number, pressure drop, and entropy generation within a porous heat exchanger compared to the constant-property assumption, fundamentally altering optimal design parameters.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-dimensional, transient numerical model of forced convection in a channel filled with a porous medium and equipped with perforated baffles (obstacles). The governing equations for momentum, energy, and species transport will be solved using the Finite Volume Method (FVM) with the SIMPLE algorithm. Temperature-dependent correlations for nanofluid properties (e.g., for AA7072-deionized water) will be integrated. A comprehensive parametric analysis will vary nanoparticle volume fraction, porous medium permeability (Darcy number), baffle geometry, and inlet temperature to assess their impact on thermal-hydraulic performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more accurate framework for modeling nanofluid flow in porous heat exchangers, quantifying the error introduced by the constant-property assumption. It will deliver new design charts correlating variable property effects with key performance metrics like heat transfer enhancement and pumping power, directly aiding in the thermal optimization of advanced systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated analysis of temperature-dependent nanofluid properties within a porous media heat exchanger featuring perforated obstacles—a configuration studied for performance enhancement but typically with constant properties. This combines Dr. Chamkha's expertise in porous media, nanofluids, and baffle design to challenge a foundational modeling assumption.

proposals.title: Viscous Dissipation and Joule Heating Effects on Bioconvective Nanofluid Flow and Heat Transfer over a Wedge with Thermal Stratification
proposals.motivation: In thermal engineering applications involving electrically conducting nanofluids, such as in MHD-based heat exchangers or electronic cooling systems, internal heat generation mechanisms are often significant but omitted from simplified models. Viscous dissipation becomes crucial in high-shear flows, while Joule heating is non-negligible under applied magnetic fields. Pre-2026 literature on stratified bioconvective flows, like Chamkha's recent work on AA7072-water nanofluid, typically neglects these combined effects, potentially leading to inaccurate thermal performance predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the simultaneous inclusion of viscous dissipation and Joule heating will significantly alter the thermal boundary layer structure, Nusselt number, and bioconvection stability in a thermally stratified, magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) nanofluid flow over a wedge, with the effect being strongly dependent on nanoparticle concentration and stratification parameters.
proposals.method: The mathematical model will extend the recent work on AA7072-deionized water nanofluid flow over a wedge by incorporating viscous dissipation (μ(∂u/∂y)^2) and Joule heating (σB0^2 u^2) terms into the energy equation. The governing PDEs (continuity, momentum, energy, concentration, and microorganism density) will be transformed using similarity transformations appropriate for the wedge flow. The resulting coupled, nonlinear ODE system will be solved numerically using the fourth-fifth order Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg (RKF45) method with a shooting technique, implemented in a computational code (e.g., MATLAB) to analyze key parameters like Eckert number (Ec), Hartmann number (Ha), and thermal stratification parameter.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more complete physical model for predicting heat transfer in stratified, bioconvective MHD nanofluid flows, directly applicable to the design of advanced thermal management systems. It will yield new benchmark data on how the interplay of viscous dissipation and Joule heating impacts the Nusselt number and the density of motile microorganisms, which is critical for bio-inspired cooling and microbial fuel cell applications.
proposals.novelty: While viscous dissipation and Joule heating have been studied separately in simpler flows, their combined investigation within the specific, complex context of a thermally stratified, bioconvective nanofluid flow over a wedge geometry—a configuration central to Chamkha's recent work—is novel and addresses a clear gap in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Transient Magnetohydrodynamic Flow and Heat Transfer of a Hybrid Nanofluid over a Pulsating Stretching Sheet with Thermal Radiation and Chemical Reaction
proposals.motivation: Most prior analyses of boundary layer flow over stretching sheets assume steady-state conditions, which is a significant simplification of many real-world industrial and engineering processes. Pre-2026 research by Chamkha and others on nanofluids, porous media, and bioconvection has highlighted the need to model time-dependent phenomena, such as start-up flows, operational transients, or pulsating manufacturing processes, to accurately predict heat and mass transfer performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing unsteadiness (via a pulsating sheet velocity or time-dependent thermal boundary condition) will significantly alter the thermal boundary layer structure and Nusselt number compared to steady-state predictions, with the effects being non-linearly modulated by the presence of hybrid nanoparticles and magnetic fields.
proposals.method: The governing partial differential equations for momentum, energy, and concentration will be derived for an unsteady, two-dimensional, incompressible flow of a water-based hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072 and TiO2) incorporating thermal radiation, chemical reaction, and a transverse magnetic field. The time-dependent similarity transformation will be applied to convert the PDEs into a system of coupled nonlinear ordinary differential equations with time as a parameter. This system will be solved numerically using the robust bvp4c solver in MATLAB, combined with a finite-difference-based temporal discretization scheme (like the Keller-box method) to handle the transient evolution from initial to periodic or steady state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis of transient hybrid nanofluid flow over a pulsating sheet, quantifying the time lag in thermal response and its dependence on key parameters. It will deliver new correlations for time-averaged Nusselt and Sherwood numbers, offering practical design insights for enhanced thermal management in dynamic systems like pulsating heat exchangers or material processing lines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic investigation of pulsating boundary conditions with a hybrid nanofluid in a transient magnetohydrodynamic framework, moving beyond the prevalent steady-state paradigm in stretching sheet problems. The integration of bioconvection effects from Chamkha's recent work as a possible solutal buoyancy mechanism further distinguishes this study.

proposals.title: Optimal Control of Stretching Velocity for Enhanced Thermal Management in Nanofluid Flow over a Permeable Wedge
proposals.motivation: In convective heat transfer systems, such as those involving stretching surfaces and nanofluids, the boundary condition—specifically the stretching velocity—is a critical but often fixed parameter. Pre-2026 research by Chamkha and others focuses on analyzing heat transfer for prescribed velocity profiles. Actively designing this profile to achieve a specific thermal outcome, like uniform surface temperature or maximized heat extraction, remains an open challenge with significant implications for industrial heat exchanger and material processing design.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying optimal control theory to the boundary condition of a stretching surface will yield a non-trivial, time- or space-dependent velocity profile that can significantly enhance thermal performance metrics, such as Nusselt number or temperature uniformity, compared to standard linear or power-law stretching.
proposals.method: The method will formulate a constrained optimization problem where the governing PDEs (continuity, momentum, energy) for a nanofluid flow over a permeable wedge, incorporating bioconvection and stratification effects, serve as state equations. The stretching velocity at the boundary will be the control variable. The Pontryagin's Maximum Principle (PMP) will be applied to derive the necessary optimality conditions, leading to a two-point boundary value problem. This system will be solved numerically using a shooting method coupled with the Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg (RKF45) scheme, iterating until the objective (e.g., minimizing a thermal performance functional) is achieved.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for the active design of boundary conditions in convective heat transfer, moving beyond analysis to synthesis. It will yield specific, optimized velocity profiles for target thermal outcomes in complex nanofluid systems, offering a new tool for thermal engineers. The results will establish a direct link between control theory objectives and practical heat transfer enhancement.
proposals.novelty: While control theory is established in fluid mechanics (e.g., for drag reduction), its application to directly design the fundamental stretching boundary condition for thermal optimization in nanofluid-bioconvection systems is novel. This approach treats the velocity profile not as a given but as a design degree of freedom, a paradigm shift from prior analytical studies.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Stretching Sheet Velocity for Prescribed Thermal Management in Nanofluid Flow: A Hybrid Numerical Approach
proposals.motivation: In industrial applications like polymer extrusion, metal spinning, and cooling of continuous strips, achieving a precise temperature distribution is critical for product quality. While the forward problem of predicting temperature for a given stretching velocity is well-studied, the inverse problem of determining the required velocity profile for a target thermal state remains a significant challenge. Solving this inverse problem would enable the active design of manufacturing processes for optimal thermal control, a capability not yet fully realized in pre-2026 literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a specified temperature distribution along a stretching surface, a unique and physically attainable velocity profile can be determined by reformulating the governing boundary layer equations as an inverse problem, solvable via a stable hybrid numerical scheme.
proposals.method: The method begins with the standard conservation equations for momentum, energy, and nanoparticle concentration for a nanofluid flow over a stretching sheet. The forward model will be solved using a robust finite difference scheme with appropriate boundary conditions. For the inverse problem, the target temperature profile will be treated as an additional boundary condition. We will employ the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm, a stable gradient-based optimization method, to iteratively adjust the unknown velocity profile parameters until the computed temperature field matches the target distribution, minimizing a predefined error functional. Sensitivity analysis will be conducted to assess the stability of the solution against measurement noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for the inverse thermal design of stretching sheet processes, directly linking desired thermal outcomes to required operational parameters. It will extend the applicability of boundary layer flow analysis from predictive simulation to active process design, offering a new tool for thermal engineers. The results will also reveal the functional relationship between complex velocity fields and resultant thermal signatures in nanofluid systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the classic Sakiadis flow problem for nanofluids, shifting from analysis to design. While Chamkha's prior work extensively analyzes forward heat transfer with various parameters, this proposal uniquely seeks to determine the causative velocity field for a prescribed thermal effect, a non-trivial inverse engineering problem.

proposals.title: Dual Solutions and Thermal Transport in a Nanofluid Flow Induced by a Permeable Shrinking Sheet with Chemical Reaction and Bioconvection
proposals.motivation: While flow over a stretching sheet is extensively studied, its dual problem—flow over a shrinking sheet—presents unique physical challenges, including boundary layer behavior and potential non-uniqueness of solutions, which are less explored. Investigating this configuration is crucial for applications like material processing, polymer sheet shrinkage, and specific microfluidic manipulations where convergent surface motion dominates. Incorporating contemporary complexities like nanofluids, bioconvection, and chemical reactions, as seen in Dr. Chamkha's recent work, will bridge a significant gap in the literature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the flow induced by a permeable shrinking sheet will exhibit dual (multiple) steady-state solutions for a range of physical parameters, and that the inclusion of nanoparticles and gyrotactic microorganisms will significantly alter the heat and mass transfer characteristics compared to the classical stretching sheet case.
proposals.method: The mathematical model will be formulated using boundary layer theory, incorporating continuity, momentum, energy, concentration, and microorganism density equations for a water-based nanofluid. The governing partial differential equations will be transformed into a system of coupled, non-linear ordinary differential equations using similarity transformations. This system will be solved numerically using the bvp4c solver in MATLAB, a robust algorithm for boundary value problems, to accurately capture dual solutions. A parametric analysis will be performed by varying key parameters: suction/injection strength, nanoparticle volume fraction, chemical reaction parameter, bioconvection parameters, and the Lewis and Peclet numbers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive analysis of the existence and stability of dual flow solutions for the shrinking sheet problem with modern enhancements. It will deliver new insights into how synergistic effects of nanoparticles, chemical reaction, and bioconvection modulate heat and mass transfer rates, quantified through local Nusselt, Sherwood, and density numbers. The results will serve as a benchmark for future studies on complex flows with converging boundaries.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated study of a shrinking sheet with a permeable surface, a chemically reactive nanofluid, and bioconvection—a combination not previously addressed. The explicit focus on identifying and characterizing the dual solution space for this specific physical configuration moves beyond the well-trodden stretching sheet paradigm.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Optimization of Perforated Inner Body Geometry and Placement in a Nanofluid-Filled Porous Enclosure for Enhanced Thermal Performance
proposals.motivation: Enhancing heat transfer in enclosures is critical for improving the efficiency of thermal systems like heat exchangers and electronic cooling. While the use of inner bodies (obstacles) and nanofluids in porous media are established enhancement techniques, their combined optimization is not fully explored. A systematic study linking the shape, size, and position of perforated obstacles to the convective dynamics of nanofluids in porous media is needed to provide actionable design guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal, non-intuitive configuration of perforated inner bodies—defined by their shape, size, and placement—exists that maximizes the overall heat transfer rate in a nanofluid-saturated porous enclosure by optimally disrupting and guiding the flow to enhance mixing and reduce thermal boundary layer thickness.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model based on the Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer formulation for porous media flow and a single-phase model for Al2O3-water nanofluid. The geometry of a single, centrally placed, perforated obstacle will be parameterized. A multi-objective genetic algorithm (NSGA-II) will be coupled with the CFD solver to optimize the obstacle's shape (via NURBS), size, and position, simultaneously maximizing Nusselt number and minimizing pressure drop. The thermal and hydraulic performance of the optimized design will be compared against standard solid and perforated geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, optimized design for perforated inner bodies tailored for nanofluid flow in porous media, directly contributing to the design of advanced compact heat exchangers. It will yield a comprehensive set of correlations linking geometric parameters to thermal-hydraulic performance metrics, offering a practical tool for thermal engineers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, automated optimization of multiple geometric degrees of freedom (shape, size, placement) for a perforated obstacle within the specific context of nanofluid flow through a porous medium, moving beyond parametric studies of fixed geometries.

proposals.title: Inverse Design of Porous Media Geometry and Hybrid Nanofluid Composition for a Target Thermal Field in a Bioconvective Flow Regime
proposals.motivation: Advanced thermal management systems, such as microchannel heat exchangers and solar collectors, require precise control of temperature distributions to optimize efficiency and component safety. Current design practices rely on iterative forward simulations, which are computationally expensive and do not guarantee an optimal solution for a specific thermal target. An inverse methodology that directly determines the required system parameters from a desired thermal field would revolutionize the design process for thermal systems utilizing nanofluids and porous structures.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given bioconvective flow regime over a wedge, a coupled inverse algorithm can uniquely determine the optimal combination of porous obstacle geometry (e.g., perforation size and distribution) and hybrid nanofluid properties (e.g., nanoparticle volume fractions and types) to achieve a pre-specified, non-uniform target temperature field.
proposals.method: The inverse problem will be formulated using a Levenberg-Marquardt optimization algorithm coupled with a direct solver. The forward model will be based on the governing equations for momentum, energy, and mass transfer for a thermally stratified, bioconvective flow of a hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-deionized water) through a porous medium over a wedge. The algorithm will iteratively adjust the design parameters—porous obstacle geometry descriptors and nanoparticle concentrations—to minimize the sum of squared differences between the computed and target temperature fields. Sensitivity analysis will be performed to identify the most influential parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for the inverse thermal design of systems employing nanofluids and porous media. It will yield specific design guidelines linking target thermal performance to manufacturable geometric features and nanoparticle mixtures. The methodology will be a significant step beyond current forward-analysis-based design, offering a direct path to optimized thermal systems for energy applications.
proposals.novelty: While inverse heat transfer problems exist, the simultaneous optimization of both porous media geometry and hybrid nanofluid composition for a complex, stratified bioconvective flow represents a novel and non-trivial extension. This integrates Dr. Chamkha's expertise in nanofluids, porous media, and bioconvection into a unified inverse design paradigm not previously explored in his published work.

proposals.title: Thermal Enhancement and Flow Dynamics of Hybrid Nanofluids in a Rotating Disc System with Porous Media and Chemical Reaction
proposals.motivation: Rotating disc systems are fundamental to numerous industrial applications, including heat exchangers, turbomachinery, and chemical reactors. Prior studies on flow and heat transfer between discs often assume stationary boundaries, which is a significant simplification. Investigating the more realistic case of rotating discs, particularly with modern heat transfer enhancement techniques like nanofluids and porous inserts, is crucial for optimizing the thermal performance of such systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the introduction of co- or counter-rotation of the discs, combined with a hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-deionized water) and a porous interfacial layer, will significantly alter the velocity and thermal boundary layers, leading to a non-linear enhancement in heat transfer rates and a complex interaction with solutal and chemical reaction effects.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mathematical modeling approach based on the principles of continuum mechanics and similarity transformations. Governing equations for momentum, energy, and concentration for a steady, laminar flow will be derived, incorporating the effects of disc rotation, a porous medium (via the Darcy-Forchheimer model), nanoparticles (using the Tiwari-Das model), and a chemical reaction. The resulting system of coupled, non-linear ordinary differential equations will be solved numerically using the robust bvp4c algorithm in MATLAB, with a focus on analyzing key parameters like rotation ratio, porosity, nanoparticle volume fraction, and chemical reaction rate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive parametric analysis of a novel, more physically realistic rotating disc configuration. It will deliver new correlations and insights into how rotation couples with nanofluid and porous media effects to control heat and mass transfer, directly informing the design of advanced thermal management systems. The findings will extend the existing literature on stationary disc models to dynamic operational conditions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous consideration of disc rotation, a hybrid nanofluid, and a porous fractal interfacial layer within a single chemical reaction framework, moving beyond the common and limiting assumption of stationary discs prevalent in many prior analyses.

proposals.title: Thermal Performance Analysis of Nanofluid Flow in a Porous Heat Exchanger with Temperature-Dependent Thermophysical Properties
proposals.motivation: Most studies on nanofluids for heat exchanger applications assume constant thermophysical properties, which is a significant simplification. In real systems, the thermal conductivity and viscosity of nanofluids are highly temperature-dependent, directly impacting flow dynamics, pumping power, and convective heat transfer coefficients. A model incorporating this dependence is crucial for accurate performance prediction and optimal design of thermal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating temperature-dependent thermal conductivity and viscosity into the modeling of nanofluid flow through a porous heat exchanger will reveal significant deviations in Nusselt number, friction factor, and thermal performance criteria compared to constant-property models, leading to more accurate design guidelines.
proposals.method: We will develop a mathematical model for forced convection of a metallic-oxide/water nanofluid in a channel filled with a porous medium and equipped with perforated baffles. The temperature-dependent properties will be modeled using established empirical correlations (e.g., for viscosity: Brinkman model with temperature correction; for conductivity: Maxwell model with a temperature function). The governing equations (mass, momentum, energy) will be solved numerically using the Finite Volume Method (FVM) with the SIMPLE algorithm. A parametric analysis will vary nanoparticle volume fraction (1-4%), pore density, and inlet temperature to assess their combined effect on key performance parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more realistic assessment of nanofluid-enhanced heat exchanger performance under practical operating conditions. It will yield new correlations or performance maps that integrate variable property effects, offering engineers a superior tool for system design and optimization compared to existing constant-property analyses.
proposals.novelty: While variable properties have been studied in simple channels, their integrated effect in the complex, coupled physical context of nanofluid flow through a porous matrix with engineered perforated obstacles—a hallmark of Chamkha's recent work—remains unexplored and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Transient Thermal-Hydraulic Analysis of Nanofluid Flow Between Porous Discs: Effects of Unsteady Injection/Suction and Nanoparticle Shape
proposals.motivation: Most studies on heat transfer in porous media and nanofluids, including flow between discs, assume steady-state conditions. However, practical applications like thermal energy storage, regenerative heat exchangers, and electronic cooling involve transient startup, shutdown, or pulsatile flows. Pre-2026 literature lacks a detailed analysis of how unsteady boundary conditions affect the coupled heat and flow dynamics in such configurations, which is critical for optimizing transient thermal performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unsteady injection/suction at the porous discs will induce time-dependent thermal and hydrodynamic boundary layers that significantly alter the Nusselt number and skin friction compared to steady-state predictions, and that nanoparticle shape (e.g., blades, cylinders, platelets) will have a pronounced, time-modulated effect on this transient behavior.
proposals.method: We will develop an unsteady, axisymmetric mathematical model for nanofluid flow between two coaxially placed porous discs with time-dependent injection/suction velocities. The governing partial differential equations (continuity, momentum, energy) will be non-dimensionalized and solved using the explicit finite difference method (FDM) with a stability-controlled time-stepping algorithm. The Tiwari-Das nanofluid model will be employed to incorporate the effects of different nanoparticle shapes (using shape factors from pre-2026 literature) and volume fractions. Key parameters analyzed will include the unsteadiness parameter, injection/suction Reynolds number, porosity, and nanoparticle shape factor.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive parametric study of transient heat transfer and flow characteristics for nanofluids in a porous disc system. It will yield novel insights into the time scales required to reach thermal equilibrium and produce practical correlations for time-dependent Nusselt number as a function of the governing parameters, directly useful for the design of advanced thermal management systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the confluence of three pre-2026 research threads in a transient framework: unsteady porous media flow, shape-dependent nanofluid properties, and the specific geometry of disc-based exchangers, which has previously been analyzed almost exclusively for steady-state conditions.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Optimization of Large-Scale Disc-Shaped Heat Exchangers Using Nanofluid-Enhanced Fractal Porous Media Models
proposals.motivation: Industrial heat exchangers face escalating demands for compactness and efficiency, particularly in high-power applications like concentrated solar power and advanced electronics cooling. Scaling up promising lab-scale designs, such as those with perforated obstacles or nanofluids, introduces complex, non-linear thermo-fluid interactions that are poorly predicted by conventional correlations, leading to suboptimal performance and increased material costs.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a fractal interfacial layer model for nanoparticle transport within a porous media framework will accurately capture the dominant heat and mass transfer mechanisms in scaled-up disc-shaped geometries, enabling the identification of optimal design parameters that maximize thermal performance while minimizing pressure drop.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D conjugate heat transfer model in a commercial CFD solver (e.g., ANSYS Fluent) incorporating a user-defined function for the fractal nanofluid model from Chamkha's prior work. The disc geometry will be treated as a porous region with variable permeability, simulating perforated obstacles. A multi-objective genetic algorithm (NSGA-II) will be employed to optimize key parameters: nanoparticle concentration (AA7072/water), fractal layer dimension, pore density, and flow channel configuration. The objective functions will be Nusselt number enhancement and a dimensionless pumping power penalty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable design methodology for high-performance disc heat exchangers, directly linking micro-scale nanoparticle phenomena to macro-scale system performance. It will yield a set of Pareto-optimal design charts and correlations for industrial engineers, bridging the gap between advanced nanofluid research and practical thermal system design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of a fractal nanofluid model with porous media flow in a large-scale, non-standard (disc-shaped) geometry for systematic industrial design optimization, a combination not explored in pre-2026 literature which typically treats these aspects in isolation or at lab scale.

proposals.title: Multi-Objective Optimization of Heat Transfer in a Porous Enclosure with Hybrid Nanofluids: A Response Surface Methodology and Genetic Algorithm Approach
proposals.motivation: Enhancing heat transfer in thermal systems like heat exchangers is critical for energy efficiency. While studies on nanofluids and porous media are abundant, a systematic optimization framework to maximize the Nusselt number by simultaneously tuning multiple flow and material parameters is lacking. This gap limits the practical design of high-performance thermal systems based on predictive models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a coupled computational and optimization approach can identify a non-intuitive set of optimal parameters (e.g., nanoparticle concentration, Darcy number, Reynolds number) that synergistically maximize convective heat transfer in a defined geometry, surpassing performance predicted by single-parameter studies.
proposals.method: The study will model conjugate heat transfer in a porous enclosure with a hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-deionized water) using a finite volume solver. Key parameters (nanoparticle volume fraction, Darcy number, Reynolds number, geometry of internal obstacles) will be varied to generate a dataset of Nusselt numbers. Response Surface Methodology (RSM) will construct a surrogate model, which will then be optimized using a multi-objective Genetic Algorithm (NSGA-II) to maximize Nusselt number while potentially minimizing pumping power.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust optimization framework for thermal system design, yielding Pareto-optimal solutions for heat transfer enhancement. It will deliver specific, actionable parameter sets for engineers and advance the methodology for tackling coupled thermo-fluid problems beyond isolated parametric analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated application of RSM and GA specifically to the dual problem of maximizing Nusselt number in a porous medium with chemically reactive hybrid nanofluids, moving beyond conventional single-variable analysis to uncover complex multi-parameter interactions.

proposals.title: Analysis of Radiative Entropy Generation in High-Temperature Nanofluid Flow Through Porous Heat Exchangers
proposals.motivation: In high-temperature applications like concentrated solar power receivers or advanced nuclear cooling, nanofluids are used to enhance convective heat transfer. However, the common analytical simplification of neglecting radiative heat transfer becomes invalid at elevated temperatures, potentially leading to significant errors in thermal performance and irreversibility predictions. A comprehensive model integrating radiation is essential for accurate system design and optimization of second-law efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that including radiative heat transfer and its associated entropy generation will significantly alter the total irreversibility and optimal operating parameters in high-temperature nanofluid systems, rendering the 'negligible radiation' assumption inadequate for thermal design.
proposals.method: The study will employ a numerical finite-volume method to solve the coupled conservation equations for momentum, energy, and species transport for a nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-water) flowing through a porous medium. The radiative transfer equation (RTE) will be solved using the discrete ordinates method (DOM) to model thermal radiation. Local entropy generation due to fluid friction, heat conduction, mass diffusion, and radiative transfer will be calculated.
