Key parameters like nanoparticle volume fraction, porous medium permeability, surface emissivity, and optical thickness will be varied to perform a detailed parametric analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for second-law analysis of high-temperature nanofluid systems that accounts for radiative entropy generation. It will yield new correlations or guidelines for identifying the temperature and geometry regimes where radiation must be included, directly aiding the thermal design of more efficient industrial heat exchangers and energy systems.
proposals.novelty: While entropy generation analysis of nanofluids in porous media exists, the explicit integration of radiative entropy generation within such a framework, particularly for high-temperature applications with metallic nanoparticles, is a novel and necessary advancement beyond current pre-2026 studies that often omit this effect.

proposals.title: Local Thermal Non-Equilibrium Analysis of Nanofluid Flow and Heat Transfer in a Porous Wavy Cylinder with Bioconvection Effects
proposals.motivation: The analysis of heat transfer in porous media often assumes local thermal equilibrium (LTE) between the solid matrix and the fluid, which fails for high-Reynolds-number flows, large property differences, or applications like compact heat exchangers. Wavy surfaces are established passive enhancers of thermal performance, and their combination with porous inserts and nanofluids is a promising yet complex configuration. A rigorous LTNE model is required to accurately predict the distinct temperature fields in the solid and fluid phases, which is critical for the optimal design of advanced thermal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying local thermal non-equilibrium (LTNE) conditions to model a porous wavy cylinder with nanofluid flow will reveal significant temperature differences between the solid and fluid phases, fundamentally altering the predicted heat transfer characteristics compared to the standard LTE assumption, especially when combined with bioconvection and chemical reaction effects.
proposals.method: The study will employ a numerical finite volume method to solve the coupled governing equations for momentum, energy (two-equation LTNE model), mass transfer, and bioconvection for a steady, laminar flow. The geometry will be a vertical cylinder with a sinusoidal wavy surface, saturated with a water-based nanofluid (e.g., AA7072 nanoparticles) and a porous medium. The Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer model will govern flow in the porous region. Key parameters to be varied include the wavy surface amplitude, Biot number, porosity, nanoparticle volume fraction, and chemical reaction rate. The SIMPLE algorithm will handle pressure-velocity coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed parametric analysis of the interplay between wavy geometry, LTNE, nanofluid properties, and bioconvection on Nusselt number and skin friction. It will yield new correlations or guidelines for designing porous wavy heat exchangers operating under non-equilibrium conditions. The results will extend the understanding of complex multi-physics transport in engineered porous structures.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous integration of three advanced concepts—a wavy cylindrical geometry, a two-temperature LTNE model for the porous medium, and bioconvective nanofluid flow—into a single, coherent computational framework, which has not been extensively studied in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Transient Entropy Generation and Heat Transfer Analysis of Hybrid Nanofluids in a Porous Enclosure with Perforated Baffles
proposals.motivation: The design of efficient thermal management systems, such as compact heat exchangers and electronic cooling devices, requires a deep understanding of unsteady thermal-fluid behavior. Pre-2026 studies on nanofluids and porous media often focus on steady-state performance, neglecting the critical start-up and dynamic response phases where entropy generation and irreversibilities are highly time-dependent. Analyzing these transient regimes is essential for optimizing system control and minimizing thermodynamic losses in practical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the time-dependent evolution of heat transfer and entropy generation in a hybrid nanofluid-filled porous enclosure is non-monotonic and can be optimized by strategically configuring perforated baffles. The transient peaks in Nusselt number and entropy generation rates will be strongly influenced by the interaction between nanoparticle thermophysical properties, porous matrix inertia, and the unsteady wake dynamics past the perforations.
proposals.method: The study will employ a numerical finite volume method to solve the unsteady, two-dimensional governing equations for mass, momentum, and energy conservation, incorporating the Tiwari-Das nanofluid model and the Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer model for porous media. A hybrid nanofluid of AA7072 and Cu nanoparticles in a water base will be modeled. The geometry will be a square enclosure with a hot left wall and a cold right wall, containing a centered, adiabatic perforated baffle (with porosity and hole pattern as key parameters). The SIMPLE algorithm will handle pressure-velocity coupling, and a second-order implicit scheme will manage temporal discretization. The Bejan number and volumetric entropy generation rate will be computed throughout the transient simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed map of the transient thermal and irreversibility characteristics for a complex hybrid nanofluid-porous-baffle system, which has not been comprehensively studied. It will yield practical guidelines for baffle design (porosity, placement) and nanoparticle selection to minimize the time to reach thermal steady-state while controlling entropy production. The results will advance the foundational knowledge for designing next-generation adaptive thermal systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the coupled, time-resolved investigation of entropy generation and heat transfer for a hybrid nanofluid within a porous medium dynamically interacting with a perforated obstacle—a synthesis of Chamkha's key pre-2026 research themes. Most prior analyses treat these elements (perforations, nanoparticles, porous media) in isolation or under steady-state assumptions.

proposals.title: Radiative Heat Transfer Analysis in a Porous Medium with AA7072-Water Nanofluid Flow and Bioconvection over a Wedge
proposals.motivation: Prior studies by Chamkha and others on nanofluid flow with bioconvection and chemical reactions often neglect thermal radiation, a significant mode of energy transfer in high-temperature applications like solar collectors, nuclear reactors, and spacecraft thermal management. Incorporating radiation effects is crucial for accurate thermal performance prediction, especially when using advanced nanofluids (e.g., AA7072-deionized water) in porous media, which are common in modern heat exchangers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating thermal radiation via the Rosseland diffusion approximation will significantly alter the thermal boundary layer structure, enhance the effective thermal conductivity, and improve the overall heat transfer rate in the stratified, bioconvective nanofluid flow system over a wedge.
proposals.method: The governing equations for momentum, energy, concentration, and microorganism density will be extended by adding the radiative heat flux term to the energy equation. The Rosseland approximation for an optically thick medium will be employed to model thermal radiation. The nonlinear PDEs will be transformed using similarity transformations, based on pre-2026 methods, and solved numerically via the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method with a shooting technique. A parametric analysis will investigate the influence of the radiation parameter (Rd), nanoparticle volume fraction, stratification parameters, bioconvection parameters, and chemical reaction on Nusselt number, skin friction, and Sherwood number.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more comprehensive model for high-temperature nanofluid flows in porous media, quantifying the synergistic effects of radiation, bioconvection, and nanoparticle dispersion. It will deliver new correlations and benchmark data for engineers designing advanced thermal systems where radiation is non-negligible.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the simultaneous integration of thermal radiation, solutal/thermal stratification, bioconvection, and chemical reaction for a metallic-nanomaterial (AA7072) nanofluid within a porous medium over a wedge geometry, a combination not extensively studied in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Coupled Lorentz-Porous Force Manipulation via a Functionally Graded Medium on a Riga Plate: A Numerical Study for Active Boundary Layer Control
proposals.motivation: The Riga plate, generating an exponentially decaying Lorentz force, is a recognized actuator for boundary layer flow control and heat transfer enhancement. However, its interaction with porous media, typically modeled as homogeneous, is passive and suboptimal. Integrating a functionally graded porous medium (FGPM) with a tailored spatial permeability profile offers a novel pathway to actively shape the combined electromagnetic and porous drag force field, enabling precise control over flow and thermal characteristics for advanced thermal management systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically grading the porosity and permeability of the porous medium adjacent to the Riga plate will create a synergistic coupling with the plate's Lorentz force, allowing for targeted manipulation of the velocity and thermal boundary layers, which cannot be achieved with uniform porous media or the Lorentz force alone.
proposals.method: The study will develop a coupled numerical model based on the extended Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer formulation for the FGPM and the modified Grinberg-Termitis model for the Riga plate's Lorentz force. The spatial grading of permeability (e.g., K(y) = K_0 * exp(-βy) or power-law variations) will be explicitly incorporated. The governing equations for momentum, energy, and mass transfer will be solved using the finite volume method (FVM) in ANSYS Fluent or a custom code, implementing the SIMPLE algorithm for pressure-velocity coupling. A parametric analysis will optimize grading parameters (β, exponent) against objectives like Nusselt number maximization or shear stress uniformity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis of the non-linear interaction between a traveling-wave Lorentz force and a non-homogeneous porous drag. It will deliver an optimization framework for designing FGPMs to achieve specific engineering outcomes, such as suppressing flow instabilities or creating customized heat transfer profiles, directly contributing to the design of next-generation active heat exchangers and flow control devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating the porous medium not as a passive filler but as a functionally graded component whose properties are explicitly designed to couple with and enhance the active control capability of the Riga plate's unique Lorentz force distribution, a concept not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Slip Flow and Thermal Jump Effects on Bioconvective Heat and Mass Transfer of AA7072-Water Nanofluid over a Stratified Wedge
proposals.motivation: The no-slip boundary condition is a standard simplification, but it fails for microscale/nanoscale flows, superhydrophobic surfaces, and rarefied gases, where velocity slip and temperature jump are significant. Incorporating these conditions is crucial for accurately modeling advanced thermal systems, such as micro-channel heat exchangers and coatings using nanofluids, which are active research areas in heat transfer. This study addresses a gap in the analysis of complex bioconvective flows with stratification under more realistic boundary conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing velocity slip and thermal jump boundary conditions will significantly alter the velocity, temperature, and concentration profiles, leading to quantifiable changes in the local Nusselt and Sherwood numbers for the stratified, bioconvective nanofluid flow over a wedge. The slip parameters will provide critical control mechanisms for heat and mass transfer rates.
proposals.method: The mathematical model will extend the recent work on thermal/solutal stratified Heimanz flow of AA7072-water nanofluid over a wedge with bioconvection. The governing PDEs (continuity, momentum, energy, concentration, and microorganism density) will be transformed using similarity transformations. The no-slip and no-jump conditions will be replaced with Navier's linear slip for velocity and Smoluchowski's temperature jump condition. The resulting nonlinear ODE system will be solved numerically using the fourth-order Runge-Kutta method combined with a shooting technique, implemented in MATLAB. A parametric analysis will systematically vary the slip coefficient, thermal jump coefficient, stratification parameters, bioconvection Rayleigh number, and nanoparticle volume fraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, comprehensive dataset on the interplay between slip/jump conditions and bioconvection in stratified nanofluid flows, directly applicable to the design of enhanced heat exchangers and microbial fuel cells. It will yield new correlations for engineering parameters (skin friction, Nusselt number) under slip regimes, advancing the fundamental understanding of transport phenomena at interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While slip flow has been studied in simpler geometries, its integration into a complex, multi-physics scenario combining metallic nanofluids (AA7072), double stratification, chemical reaction, and bioconvection over a wedge is novel. The simultaneous consideration of velocity slip and thermal jump within this specific configuration has not been explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Non-Linear Rosseland Approximation for Radiative Heat Flux: Analogy from Non-Newtonian Fluid Dynamics and Application to Nanofluid Flow in Porous Media
proposals.motivation: The Rosseland diffusion approximation, a cornerstone for modeling radiative heat transfer in optically thick media, assumes a linear relationship between radiative flux and temperature gradient. This linearity breaks down in systems with extreme temperature gradients, such as in laser-material processing, astrophysical plasmas, and advanced nuclear reactors, leading to significant prediction errors. A robust, non-linear model for radiative conductivity is needed to improve accuracy in these high-flux regimes, which are increasingly relevant in advanced thermal engineering and energy systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by drawing an analogy to non-Newtonian power-law fluid models, where effective viscosity depends on shear rate, the radiative conductivity can be effectively modeled as a function of both temperature and the magnitude of the temperature gradient (k_rad = k_0 T^n |∇T|^(m-1)). This will yield a more accurate, non-linear Rosseland flux law for predicting heat transfer in systems where standard radiative diffusion fails.
proposals.method: We will formulate a generalized radiative flux law, q_rad = -k_0 T^n |∇T|^(m-1) ∇T, where parameters n and m are determined via analogy to established non-Newtonian models (e.g., Ostwald-de Waele power-law). This constitutive relation will be integrated into the energy equation for a benchmark problem: the flow and heat transfer of a metallic-nanoparticle nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-water) in a porous medium with thermal stratification and bioconvection, extending our prior work. The resulting strongly non-linear PDE system will be solved numerically using a finite difference method with an iterative solver to handle the non-linearity, analyzing the impact on Nusselt number and temperature profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-inspired framework for non-linear radiative diffusion, directly applicable to computational fluid dynamics (CFD) codes for high-temperature processes. It will deliver new insights into the coupled heat transfer mechanisms in complex nanofluid-porous systems under intense thermal radiation, potentially revealing enhanced or suppressed heat transfer rates compared to linear models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual transfer of a non-Newtonian constitutive model to radiative heat flux, creating a non-linear Rosseland approximation—a significant departure from the universally applied linear model in pre-2026 literature. The application of this model to stratified nanofluid bioconvection in porous media combines several of our group's expertise areas into a unique test case.

proposals.title: A 3D Conjugate Heat Transfer FEM Framework with Integrated Non-Linear Rosseland Radiative Flux for High-Temperature Porous and Nanofluid Systems
proposals.motivation: Accurate thermal simulation of high-temperature systems like solar receivers, nuclear reactor cores, and industrial furnaces is critical for efficiency and safety. Pre-2026, modeling non-linear radiative heat transfer in such optically thick, complex geometries (e.g., porous heat exchangers, nanofluid-cooled components) remains computationally prohibitive, often requiring simplified 2D models or decoupled radiation solvers that sacrifice fidelity for speed. This gap hinders the design optimization of advanced thermal systems where radiation dominates.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a temperature-dependent, non-linear Rosseland radiative conductivity directly into a commercial 3D Finite Element Method (FEM) solver for conjugate heat transfer and fluid flow will enable efficient, high-fidelity simulation of entire high-temperature thermal systems, accurately capturing the dominant T^3/T^4 radiative flux without separate radiation transport solvers.
proposals.method: The research will implement the non-linear Rosseland approximation, where radiative conductivity is k_rad = (16σ n^2 T^3)/(3β_R), as a user-defined, temperature-dependent material property within COMSOL Multiphysics. A 3D FEM model will be developed for conjugate heat transfer (fluid and solid domains) coupled with laminar/turbulent flow (k-ε model). The framework will be validated against benchmark cases (e.g., participating media in enclosures) and applied to two pre-2026 relevant systems: a porous medium heat exchanger and a channel flow of metallic-nanomaterial nanofluid under high thermal stratification. A parametric analysis will study the influence of optical properties (n, β_R), nanoparticle concentration, and geometry on the integrated radiative-convective heat transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, practical computational tool for system-level 3D thermal design where radiation is non-linear and dominant. It will provide new insights into the synergistic effects of radiation, convection, and conduction in porous and nanofluid-based systems at high temperatures. The results will directly inform the optimization of heat transfer performance in advanced energy systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, monolithic coupling of the non-linear Rosseland model within a commercial 3D FEM framework for full conjugate heat transfer, moving beyond decoupled approaches or simplified geometries. This integration for complex, real-world system simulation (e.g., combining porous media, nanofluids, and strong radiation) is not extensively reported in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Impact of Constitutive Model Selection on Heat Transfer and Flow of Non-Newtonian Nanofluids in a Porous Heat Exchanger: A Comparative CFD Study
proposals.motivation: Non-Newtonian nanofluids are promising for enhanced thermal management in industrial systems like compact heat exchangers. However, their complex rheology, described by various constitutive models (e.g., Power-law, Casson, Carreau), leads to significant uncertainty in predicting flow and heat transfer performance. A systematic, application-driven comparison of these models is lacking, hindering the optimal design of systems utilizing such advanced fluids.
proposals.hypothesis: For a defined porous heat exchanger geometry, the choice of non-Newtonian constitutive model for a nanoparticle-enhanced fluid will significantly alter the predicted Nusselt number, pressure drop, and local stress distributions, with yield-stress models showing the most deviation from generalized Newtonian predictions under low shear conditions.
proposals.method: The study will define a benchmark problem: forced convection of a shear-thinning nanofluid (e.g., Al2O3 in a polymer base) through a channel filled with a porous medium, representative of an enhanced heat exchanger core. Governing equations for momentum (incorporating Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer extension) and energy will be solved numerically using the Finite Volume Method in ANSYS Fluent or a similar CFD platform. Identical simulations will be run for a curated set of models: Power-law, Carreau, and Herschel-Bulkley. Key performance parameters (global Nusselt number, friction factor, and localized shear stress) will be extracted and compared quantitatively.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clear, quantitative framework for selecting appropriate rheological models in applied thermal-fluid studies of non-Newtonian nanofluids. It will yield actionable guidelines linking fluid characteristics (e.g., yield stress presence) to the predictive accuracy of key engineering parameters, directly aiding in the design of more efficient thermal systems.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies apply single models, this work performs a controlled, like-for-like comparative analysis of multiple constitutive laws within a unified, industrially relevant porous media transport problem, explicitly quantifying the impact of model choice on thermal-hydraulic predictions.

proposals.title: Transient Magnetohydrodynamic Flow and Heat Transfer of a Williamson Nanofluid Past a Riga Plate with Chemical Reaction and Bioconvection
proposals.motivation: The analysis of unsteady, non-Newtonian flows over specialized surfaces like the Riga plate is crucial for optimizing advanced thermal systems, including micro-coolers and bio-medical devices. Williamson fluids model shear-thinning behavior common in industrial and biological suspensions, while the Riga plate's electromagnetic actuator offers active flow control without moving parts. Pre-2026 literature lacks a comprehensive transient analysis combining these elements with nanofluids and bioconvection, which is essential for understanding start-up processes and dynamic thermal management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unsteady flow of a Williamson nanofluid past a Riga plate will exhibit distinct transient thermal and solutal boundary layers, where the combined effects of the modified Hartmann number (from the Riga plate), nanoparticle volume fraction, and bioconvection parameters will significantly alter the rate of heat and mass transfer compared to steady-state or Newtonian cases.
proposals.method: The governing partial differential equations for momentum, energy, concentration, and microorganism density will be derived using boundary layer approximations. The Cattaneo-Christov heat flux model will be incorporated for non-Fourier thermal analysis. The transient system will be solved numerically using the implicit finite-difference method, specifically the Keller-box scheme, validated against established steady-state and Newtonian limits. Key parameters to be varied include the Williamson fluid parameter, modified Hartmann number, bioconvection Peclet and Lewis numbers, chemical reaction rate, and unsteadiness parameter.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed temporal maps of velocity, temperature, nanoparticle concentration, and microorganism profiles for this complex configuration. It will quantify the transient Nusselt and Sherwood numbers, offering critical design data for engineers. The results will extend the understanding of how electromagnetic body forces interact with non-Newtonian rheology and biological transport in a dynamic regime.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the first-ever investigation of *transient* Williamson nanofluid flow over a Riga plate, synergistically integrating bioconvection and chemical reaction effects—components studied separately in Chamkha's prior steady works—into a single, time-dependent model for dynamic system analysis.

proposals.title: A Lattice Boltzmann Method Framework for Efficient 3D Conjugate Heat Transfer Simulation in Complex Geometries with Nanoparticle-Enhanced Fluids
proposals.motivation: Simulating conjugate heat transfer in complex 3D fluid-solid systems, such as advanced heat exchangers with porous inserts or nanoparticle-laden flows, is computationally intensive with traditional Navier-Stokes solvers due to meshing challenges and convergence issues. The Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBM) has proven highly effective for complex 3D flows in porous media in petroleum engineering, offering a promising, yet underexplored, alternative for thermal-fluid simulations. Adapting this robust mesoscopic approach to thermal problems can unlock efficient analysis of next-generation thermal management systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The Lattice Boltzmann Method, adapted for conjugate heat transfer with a double-population thermal model (D2Q9 for flow, D2Q9/D3Q7 for temperature), can simulate 3D heat transfer in intricate fluid-solid geometries with nanoparticle suspensions more efficiently than conventional finite volume/element methods, while accurately capturing key parameters like Nusselt number and thermal boundary layers.
proposals.method: The project will develop a 3D LBM solver, potentially extending the open-source Palabos framework, implementing a double-distribution function model for velocity and temperature fields. Boundary conditions for fluid-solid interfaces will use the bounce-back scheme for momentum and a conjugate heat transfer model for temperature continuity. The geometry will represent a complex heat exchanger channel with staggered porous fins, and the fluid will be a water-based nanofluid (e.g., Al2O3 or AA7072 nanoparticles). Simulations will be validated against established experimental or high-fidelity CFD data for simpler cases before analyzing thermal performance parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, efficient LBM-based computational tool specifically tailored for 3D conjugate heat transfer in complex geometries relevant to thermal engineering. It will deliver new insights into the synergistic effects of geometric complexity (porous media) and nanoparticle enhancement on heat transfer performance, offering design guidelines for advanced thermal systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic adaptation and validation of the LBM—a method dominant in porous media flow—for the specific, coupled problem of 3D conjugate heat transfer with nanofluids in engineered complex geometries, a direct methodological transfer not commonly explored in pre-2026 thermal engineering literature.

proposals.title: Three-Dimensional Finite Element Analysis of Enhanced Heat Transfer in a Porous Channel with Perforated Baffles and AA7072-Water Nanofluid
proposals.motivation: Enhancing thermal performance in compact heat exchangers is critical for energy efficiency in industrial systems. Prior 2D studies on perforated obstacles and nanofluids, like AA7072-water, show promise but lack the realistic 3D flow physics, vortex interactions, and conjugate heat transfer effects necessary for optimal design. A high-fidelity 3D simulation is required to bridge this gap and provide actionable design parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a 3D finite element simulation will reveal significant secondary flow structures and localized thermal stratification around perforated baffles in a porous medium, leading to a more accurate prediction of the Nusselt number enhancement for AA7072-water nanofluid compared to existing 2D models.
proposals.method: We will employ the commercial finite element software COMSOL Multiphysics (v5.6 or 6.0) to solve the coupled 3D governing equations for momentum (via Darcy-Brinkman-Forchheimer model for porous media), energy, and nanoparticle concentration. The geometry will consist of a rectangular channel with strategically placed perforated baffles. The Galerkin finite element method with P2-P1 elements for velocity-pressure and quadratic Lagrange elements for temperature will be used, implementing a segregated solver with adaptive mesh refinement near baffle surfaces to capture boundary layers accurately.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated 3D computational framework for analyzing complex nanofluid flow in porous media with engineered obstacles. It will yield detailed contour maps of thermal performance and pressure drop, leading to optimized geometric parameters (baffle porosity, spacing, nanofluid concentration) for maximal heat transfer per unit pumping power.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fully 3D, conjugate heat transfer analysis combining Chamkha's recent themes—perforated obstacles, metallic nanofluids (AA7072), and porous media flow—using a high-fidelity finite element approach, moving beyond the common 2D or simplified analytical models prevalent in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: A Hybrid CFD-Machine Learning Framework for Multi-Objective Optimization of Riga Plate Flow and Heat Transfer Using AA7072-Deionized Water Nanofluid
proposals.motivation: Riga plates, which utilize electromagnetic actuators to control boundary layer flow, offer significant potential for enhancing heat transfer in thermal systems. However, optimizing their numerous parameters (e.g., plate dimensions, magnetic field strength, nanoparticle volume fraction) for conflicting objectives like maximizing Nusselt number while minimizing skin friction is computationally expensive and complex using traditional parametric studies alone. The integration of machine learning (ML) for rapid surrogate modeling and optimization, a growing pre-2026 trend in thermal-fluid sciences, can address this bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural network-based surrogate model, trained on high-fidelity CFD data, can accurately predict the thermo-fluidic performance of a Riga plate system and be coupled with a genetic algorithm to efficiently identify Pareto-optimal parameter sets that traditional methods would overlook.
proposals.method: The methodology involves three stages. First, a validated finite volume CFD solver will generate a comprehensive dataset by simulating the flow of AA7072-deionized water nanofluid over a Riga plate under varying parameters (Hartmann number, modified Grashof number, wedge angle, nanoparticle concentration). Second, a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) neural network will be trained on this dataset to predict key outputs (Nusselt number, skin friction coefficient). Third, the trained MLP surrogate will be integrated with the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to perform multi-objective optimization, identifying optimal parameter combinations for desired thermal and hydrodynamic performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, computationally efficient hybrid framework for the design of electromagnetic flow control devices. It will provide explicit Pareto-optimal design curves for Riga plates using a specific nanofluid, offering practical guidance for engineers. The research will also demonstrate the effective application of established ML techniques (MLP, NSGA-II) to a complex, coupled thermo-magneto-hydrodynamic problem within Dr. Chamkha's research domain.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to simpler thermal systems, its integration for the multi-objective optimization of a Riga plate—considering nanofluid effects, bioconvection, and chemical reaction as per recent works—is novel. The proposed framework moves beyond single-point prediction to automated, global design space exploration for this specific, complex configuration.

proposals.title: Lattice Boltzmann Simulation of Non-Newtonian Nanofluid Flow and Heat Transfer in a Porous Media with Bioconvection
proposals.motivation: The thermal management of complex systems, such as enhanced geothermal reservoirs or advanced heat exchangers, increasingly relies on nanofluids flowing through porous structures. Pre-2026, accurately modeling the coupled micro-scale phenomena—non-Newtonian rheology, nanoparticle transport, and bioconvection in porous media—remains a significant challenge for conventional CFD solvers due to complex boundary conditions and interfacial interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-relaxation-time (MRT) lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) can effectively capture the coupled momentum, energy, and mass transport in a non-Newtonian (e.g., power-law or Carreau model) nanofluid within a porous medium, revealing how pore-scale interactions govern macroscopic heat transfer enhancement and flow stability.
proposals.method: We will develop a 2D/3D double-distribution function (DDF) LBM framework. The flow field will be solved using an MRT-LBM with a generalized Newtonian model for shear-dependent viscosity, incorporating a porosity-dependent force term (e.g., the Brinkman-Forchheimer model). A separate thermal LBM will model heat transfer, coupled with a concentration equation for nanoparticles and motile microorganisms. The code will be validated against pre-2026 benchmark studies for non-Newtonian flow and porous media convection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a high-fidelity pore-scale simulation tool for complex nanofluid-porous systems, quantifying the individual and synergistic impacts of rheology, nanoparticle loading, and bioconvection on Nusselt number and friction factor. It will deliver new correlations for heat transfer performance as a function of dimensionless parameters (e.g., power-law index, bioconvection Rayleigh number, porosity).
proposals.novelty: While LBM has been applied to nanofluids or porous media separately, its integrated application to model the triple coupling of non-Newtonian behavior, nanoparticle transport, and bioconvective instability within a porous matrix is novel and directly extends Chamkha's recent work on stratified bioconvective flows and nanofluid heat transfer.

proposals.title: Thermal Performance and Flow Analysis of Nanofluid-Enhanced Microfluidic Riga Devices with Porous Media Inserts
proposals.motivation: Microfluidic systems are critical for lab-on-a-chip applications, chemical synthesis, and biomedical diagnostics, where precise thermal management and flow control are paramount. The Riga plate, known for generating an electromagnetic-driven wall-parallel Lorentz force to manipulate boundary layers at macro-scales, has not been systematically investigated in micro-scale configurations. Scaling down this technology could enable novel active mixing and heat transfer enhancement in micro-devices without moving parts, addressing a key pre-2026 challenge in micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS) and compact heat exchangers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a micro-scale Riga plate with a porous medium and a nanoparticle-laden working fluid (nanofluid) will synergistically enhance convective heat transfer and stabilize the flow regime within a microchannel, outperforming passive microfluidic designs.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined numerical and analytical approach. A 3D computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model will be developed using the finite volume method to solve the coupled momentum, energy, and mass conservation equations for a water-based AA7072 alloy nanofluid flow in a microchannel with an integrated Riga plate. The governing equations will incorporate the Buongiorno two-phase model for nanoparticle transport, a Darcy-Forchheimer extension for the porous insert, and the modified Grinberg term for the Lorentz force. Key parameters to be varied and analyzed include the micro-Riga plate's alternating electrode/magnet spacing, nanofluid volume fraction, porous layer permeability, and the Reynolds number. Validation will be performed against established analytical solutions for simplified cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset and correlation for heat transfer and flow characteristics in microfluidic Riga devices. It will establish design guidelines for implementing active electromagnetic control in micro-systems. The findings will directly contribute to the development of next-generation, high-efficiency micro-heat exchangers and actively controlled micro-reactors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fundamental downscaling of the Riga plate concept to microfluidics and its novel integration with both porous media and chemically reactive nanofluids, creating a multi-physics system not previously studied. This combines Chamkha's expertise in complex flows, nanoparticles, and porous media analysis in a new, application-driven geometry.

proposals.title: Active Cooling of a Hybrid Nanofluid Flow Past a Riga Plate: Analysis of Thermal Performance with Stratification and Bioconvection Effects
proposals.motivation: The Riga plate, known for its electromagnetic actuator effect, has been studied primarily for boundary layer flow control and heat transfer enhancement. However, its potential as an active cooling device for high-temperature systems, such as electronic components or nuclear reactor elements, remains underexplored. This dual problem of plate cooling is critical for advancing thermal management technologies, especially when combined with modern coolants like hybrid nanofluids and complex phenomena like thermal stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Lorentz force generated by the Riga plate can be strategically tuned to actively cool a thermally stratified hybrid nanofluid flow, significantly enhancing the local Nusselt number compared to passive cooling or standard flat plates, even in the presence of bioconvection and solutal gradients.
proposals.method: The study will employ a mathematical modeling approach. Governing equations for momentum, energy, concentration, and microorganism density for a steady, incompressible flow of a water-based hybrid nanofluid (e.g., AA7072 and TiO2 nanoparticles) over a Riga plate will be formulated, incorporating thermal and solutal stratification, bioconvection, and the Lorentz body force term. The system of coupled, nonlinear PDEs will be transformed using similarity transformations and solved numerically using the bvp4c solver in MATLAB, a robust finite-difference code for boundary value problems. A parametric analysis will systematically vary key parameters (modified Hartmann number, stratification parameters, nanoparticle volume fractions, bioconvection parameters) to assess their impact on skin friction, Nusselt number, Sherwood number, and microorganism density.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive analysis of the Riga plate's active cooling capability, quantifying the trade-offs between electromagnetic control and thermal performance in stratified, bioconvective environments. It will deliver optimized parameter ranges for maximum cooling efficiency using hybrid nanofluids, offering practical design insights for next-generation thermal management systems.
proposals.novelty: While Riga plate flow has been analyzed for heat transfer enhancement, this study fundamentally re-purposes it as an active cooling device—a dual problem. The novelty lies in integrating this active cooling mechanism with a thermally stratified hybrid nanofluid and bioconvection, a combination not previously investigated in the literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Enhanced Thermal Performance of Industrial-Scale Rotating Heat Exchangers: A Combined Nanofluid-Porous Media Analysis with Entropy Generation Minimization
proposals.motivation: Rotating heat exchangers (e.g., rotary regenerators) are critical for energy recovery in gas turbines, HVAC systems, and industrial processes, where maximizing heat transfer in a compact, rotating frame is paramount. However, scaling up from laboratory models to industrial machinery introduces complex, coupled phenomena—centrifugal forces, Coriolis effects, and intensified thermal stresses—that are not fully captured by existing studies on stationary or small-scale systems. Optimizing these devices is essential for improving energy efficiency and reducing operational costs in power and process industries.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic integration of nanoparticle-enhanced fluids (nanofluids) within a functionally graded porous matrix inside the rotating channels will synergistically enhance convective heat transfer and mitigate the adverse entropy generation associated with large-scale rotation, leading to a net increase in the second-law efficiency of the exchanger.
proposals.method: The study will employ a numerical framework combining the single-phase homogeneous model for nanofluids (using AA7072-water and Al2O3-water) with the non-Darcian Forchheimer-Brinkman extended model for porous media. A control-volume based finite element method (CVFEM) will solve the coupled momentum, energy, and entropy transport equations in a 3D rotating reference frame. Key parameters will include rotational Reynolds number (1e3-1e5), nanoparticle volume fraction (0-4%), porous media permeability (Darcy number 1e-5 to 1e-2), and porosity. Performance will be evaluated via Nusselt number enhancement and Bejan number profiles for entropy generation analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive, scalable model for predicting thermo-fluid performance in industrial rotating exchangers, explicitly linking nanofluid-porous media synergy to rotational dynamics. It will yield optimized design parameters (e.g., optimal porosity gradient and nanoparticle concentration for a given rotation rate) and establish practical guidelines for entropy generation minimization, directly contributing to more efficient industrial thermal systems.
proposals.novelty: While nanofluids and porous media have been studied separately for heat transfer enhancement, their combined, tailored application within the specific high-Reynolds number, rotating flow regime of industrial-scale machinery is unexplored. The novel integration of a fractal interfacial layer model for nanofluids with a graded porous structure analysis in a rotating frame addresses a critical scale-up gap.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Effective Thermal Conductivity in Metallic-Nanomaterial Nanofluids via a Coupled Levenberg-Marquardt and CFD Approach
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of nanofluid thermal performance in heat exchangers and porous media systems is hindered by the complex dependency of effective thermal conductivity (k_eff) on nanoparticle concentration, aggregation, and interfacial layers. Existing forward models, including fractal-based ones, require precise k_eff as an input, which is challenging to measure directly in operational systems. An inverse method to deduce k_eff from readily measurable global parameters like Nusselt number would significantly enhance the design and optimization of nanofluid-based thermal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The effective thermal conductivity of a nanofluid under convective flow conditions can be robustly inferred by solving an inverse problem that minimizes the discrepancy between experimentally measured Nusselt numbers and those predicted by a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) model incorporating a fractal interfacial layer description.
proposals.method: The inverse problem will be formulated as an optimization task minimizing the sum of squared errors between experimental and simulated Nusselt numbers. A validated CFD model, based on the finite volume method, will simulate laminar forced convection of AA7072-deionized water nanofluid in a channel, incorporating the fractal interfacial layer model for nanofluid properties. The Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm, a robust gradient-based optimization technique, will be employed to iteratively adjust the k_eff parameter in the CFD model until the output matches the experimental flow and thermal data. Sensitivity analysis will be performed on key parameters like nanoparticle volume fraction and flow Reynolds number.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, non-intrusive methodology for estimating the effective transport property of nanofluids in flow conditions relevant to practical applications. It will bridge the gap between macroscopic system performance measurements and microscopic property models, offering a valuable tool for validating and refining theoretical nanofluid conductivity models. The resulting framework can be adapted for property estimation in other complex fluids within thermal systems.
proposals.novelty: While inverse heat transfer problems are established, their application to deduce the fundamental property k_eff of nanofluids—specifically by integrating a fractal interfacial layer model within the optimization loop—is novel. This moves beyond forward simulation and direct measurement, creating a hybrid experimental-computational diagnostic tool tailored for advanced nanomaterials.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Transient Surface Heat Flux in Nanofluid Flow Over a Porous Wedge Using Temperature Profile Measurements
proposals.motivation: Accurate determination of surface heat flux is critical for optimizing thermal systems like heat exchangers and solar collectors. Direct measurement is often impractical, while conventional inverse methods struggle with complex, coupled transport phenomena involving nanofluids, porous media, and bioconvection, which are active research areas in heat transfer. A robust estimation technique would enable better design and analysis of advanced thermal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hybrid inverse algorithm combining the Conjugate Gradient Method (CGM) with Bayesian parameter estimation can accurately reconstruct unknown transient surface heat flux from sparse internal temperature data in a coupled flow system, even in the presence of measurement noise and uncertain thermophysical properties.
proposals.method: The forward problem will model the conjugate heat and mass transfer for a nanofluid (e.g., AA7072-water) flow over a porous wedge, incorporating thermal stratification and bioconvection effects. For the inverse solution, the CGM with adjoint problem formulation will be employed to minimize the error between computed and simulated 'measured' temperature profiles. Sensitivity analysis will be conducted to identify optimal sensor placement, and the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm will be integrated for parameter estimation of uncertain nanofluid properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated computational framework for solving inverse heat flux problems in complex, multi-physics flows relevant to contemporary thermal engineering. It will deliver practical guidelines for sensor placement and uncertainty quantification, directly aiding the design of enhanced heat transfer systems using nanofluids and porous structures.
proposals.novelty: The application of a sophisticated inverse methodology to the highly coupled, non-linear system of nanofluid flow over a porous wedge with bioconvection and stratification, moving beyond simple geometries or pure fluids commonly studied in pre-2026 inverse heat transfer literature.

ideas.title: Dimensionless Performance Mapping and Property-Decoupling for Alternative Heat Transfer Fluids
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple fluid substitution to develop a predictive framework for heat transfer and flow performance. A core set of dimensionless numbers governing momentum and heat transfer is identified, and an experimental matrix is designed using base fluids and mixtures to independently vary these parameters. Performance metrics are measured under controlled conditions, allowing the effects of viscosity, thermal conductivity, and specific heat to be isolated. The outcome is a set of generalized correlations or performance maps that can predict system behavior for any Newtonian fluid based solely on its thermophysical properties and operating conditions.

ideas.title: Anisotropic slip and nonlinear radiation coupling in high-temperature boundary layer flow over a stretching sheet
ideas.description: This research synthesizes anisotropic hydrodynamic slip boundary conditions with a nonlinear Rosseland thermal radiation approximation to model high-temperature boundary layer flow over a stretching sheet. The core novelty is analyzing the bidirectional coupling between the anisotropic velocity field—where slip differs for streamwise and normal-to-wall directions—and the intense, nonlinear radiative heat flux generated by the high surface temperatures. This combined framework reveals how directional wall slip modulates the temperature distribution and thermal boundary layer, which in turn nonlinearly amplifies or redistributes radiative cooling effects. The study aims to derive solutions that uncover unique regimes of heat and mass transfer not predicted by models using isotropic slip or linearized radiation.

ideas.title: Active scalar management via a fixed sheet in a moving fluid: Control of heat/mass transfer from a stationary boundary in a stretching free stream
ideas.description: This study investigates the coupled fluid dynamics and scalar transport (heat or mass) generated by a moving fluid past a fixed, impermeable sheet with active scalar management. The fixed sheet is equipped with a prescribed, non-uniform temperature or concentration distribution (or flux), while the free stream undergoes a controlled stretching motion. The research will derive self-similar solutions for the velocity, thermal, and concentration boundary layers, analyzing the novel control mechanisms for scalar transfer offered by tuning the free-stream stretching rate against the fixed sheet's scalar profile. This framework provides a physically motivated model for applications like thermal management of substrates in moving fluid baths or controlled release processes in industrial coating systems.

ideas.title: Stability and Bifurcation Analysis of Dual Flow Solutions with Non-Linear Radiative Heat Transfer
ideas.description: This research investigates the stability and physical realizability of dual (or multiple) fluid flow solutions in systems where thermal radiation is modeled using a non-linear Rosseland approximation. The core novelty lies in analyzing how the strong non-linear temperature dependence of radiative flux (∼T^3 ∂T/∂y) interacts with hydrodynamic instabilities and bifurcations. By performing linear temporal stability analysis on the dual solution branches, the work will determine if the non-linear radiation term changes which branch is stable, introduces new unstable modes, or modifies critical parameters (e.g., Rayleigh or Prandtl numbers) for bifurcation. The outcome is a predictive framework for flow selection in high-temperature applications like solar receivers, nuclear reactors, and aerospace systems, where radiation is dominant and linear models are inadequate.

ideas.title: Functionally Graded Porous Medium for Tailored Lorentz-Porous Force Coupling on a Riga Plate
ideas.description: This research introduces a non-homogeneous, functionally graded porous medium to the Riga plate flow configuration, where the spatial variation in porosity and permeability is explicitly designed to interact with the plate's unique traveling-wave-like Lorentz force. The core investigation focuses on how the tailored porous structure modifies the resultant combined body force field (electromagnetic + porous drag) and its subsequent impact on boundary layer development, heat transfer, and flow stability. The study employs a coupled numerical model to optimize the porous medium's grading parameters for specific engineering objectives, such as achieving a desired wall shear stress profile or controlling the onset of instabilities. This approach ensures the porous medium is an integral, physically motivated component of the active flow control system, rather than a passive addition.

ideas.title: Inverse Estimation of Slip and Transpiration Parameters from Remote Thermal Measurements
ideas.description: This research develops an inverse methodology to estimate key boundary condition parameters—wall transpiration (suction/injection) rate, velocity slip length, and thermal jump coefficient—from measured temperature profiles in a downstream flow field. The forward model incorporates the coupled effects of slip and transpiration on convective heat transfer, making the thermal signature sensitive to all three parameters. Solving this ill-posed inverse problem allows for the remote characterization of functionalized surfaces (e.g., superhydrophobic or porous) in practical flow configurations without embedded sensors at the wall. This provides a novel non-intrusive diagnostic tool for micro/nano-fluidic systems and advanced material interfaces.

ideas.title: High-Speed Industrial Film Coating Process Simulation with Non-Linear Radiative Thermal Management
ideas.description: This research direction develops a comprehensive simulation framework for industrial-scale film coating on high-speed stretching sheets, integrating a non-linear Rosseland approximation for thermal radiation. The model captures the strong temperature dependence of radiative heat flux (T^4 behavior) during the rapid drying or curing phase, which is critical for predicting temperature gradients, thermal stress, and final film properties over large areas. This allows for the first time the design and virtual testing of industrial coating lines where radiative cooling or heating is a primary mechanism, optimizing process parameters like sheet velocity and heater settings to prevent defects. The result is a predictive tool that bridges the gap between fundamental non-linear heat transfer theory and the practical constraints of scalable manufacturing.

ideas.title: Adapt non-linear rheology models for radiative heat flux
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from non-Newtonian fluid dynamics to challenge the assumption of linearity in the Rosseland approximation for radiative heat flux. In fluid mechanics, the linear Newtonian model is generalized to power-law or other non-linear constitutive relations where the effective viscosity depends on the shear rate. Similarly, for radiative transfer in optically thick media, we propose replacing the constant radiative conductivity (k_rad ~ T^3) with a function that also depends on the magnitude of the temperature gradient, e.g., k_rad = k_rad(T, |∇T|). This creates a non-linear Rosseland flux law, q_rad ~ -k_rad(T, |∇T|) ∇T, which could more accurately model radiative transport in regimes with extreme temperature gradients, such as in astrophysical plasmas, inertial confinement fusion, or during material phase changes under intense heating, where the linear diffusion approximation breaks down.

ideas.title: Adapt Lattice Boltzmann Method from Porous Media Flow to Simulate Complex 3D Fluid-Solid Heat Transfer
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics and petroleum engineering, where the Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBM) is successfully employed in commercial and research software to simulate complex 3D fluid flows through heterogeneous porous media—a problem analogous to simulating conjugate heat transfer in intricate fluid-solid systems. The analogous solution bypasses the computational intensity of direct Navier-Stokes solvers for such geometries by using a mesoscopic, particle-based LBM approach. Adapted to the original research context, this involves employing or integrating an LBM solver (e.g., within ANSYS, COMSOL, or a dedicated open-source framework) to specifically simulate the 3D flow and coupled heat transfer. This method-transfer could provide more efficient simulation of problems with highly complex geometries or specific multiphysics phenomena where traditional FEM faces meshing or convergence challenges.

ideas.title: Comparative Analysis of Non-Newtonian Constitutive Models for Targeted Transport Phenomena
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a simple survey by conducting a principled, application-focused comparison of non-Newtonian fluid models. A specific transport problem relevant to an industrial or biological process is defined, establishing clear performance metrics. A curated set of rheological models is selected based on their ability to capture distinct, relevant features like yield stress or viscoelasticity within that context. By solving the same governing equations and boundary conditions for each model, the work systematically quantifies how the choice of constitutive law impacts predictions of flow rate, heat transfer, or stress distributions, providing actionable guidance for model selection in applied research.

ideas.title: High-Fidelity 3D FEM Simulation of Thermal Systems with Non-Linear Radiative Flux
ideas.description: This research direction integrates a non-linear Rosseland approximation for radiative heat flux directly into a commercial finite element method (FEM) framework for 3D conjugate heat transfer and fluid flow. The synergy creates a novel computational tool capable of simulating high-temperature, optically thick systems—such as advanced nuclear reactors, industrial furnaces, or re-entry vehicle components—with unprecedented efficiency and accuracy. By implementing the non-linear radiative conductivity as a temperature-dependent material property within the FEM solver, the method captures the strong T^3/T^4 dependence of radiation without resorting to computationally expensive discrete ordinates or Monte Carlo radiation sub-models. This allows for practical, high-resolution 3D simulations of entire systems where radiation is non-linear and dominant, enabling design optimization and safety analysis previously beyond reach.

ideas.title: Electromagnetic Flow Control via Bio-inspired Porous Electrode Matrix
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of a solid, impermeable active surface in Riga plate flow studies by introducing a porous medium. Drawing an analogy from electrochemical engineering, specifically fuel cell porous electrode design which solves the dual problem of fluid transport and electrical current distribution, we adapt this solution. The research proposes to design and analyze a Riga plate where the active electromagnetic surface is replaced or integrated with a porous, electrically conductive matrix. This creates a novel configuration where the Lorentz force generation is intrinsically coupled with the modified flow physics through the porous medium—investigating how porosity, permeability, and electrical properties of the matrix alter the classic boundary layer stability, heat transfer, and flow control characteristics of the Riga plate system.

ideas.title: Analyze flow with different base fluids using a pharmaceutical drug delivery analogy
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by applying an analogy from pharmaceutical drug delivery. In that field, a core problem is selecting an optimal carrier fluid (e.g., saline, lipids) to deliver an active drug, requiring systematic testing of how the carrier's properties affect pharmacokinetics and efficacy. The analogous solution is adapted here by treating the base fluid in a thermal or hydraulic system as a 'carrier' for heat or momentum. The research would systematically replace water with fluids like ethylene glycol or oils, then rigorously assess how the altered carrier properties—viscosity, specific heat, thermal conductivity, and chemical compatibility—impact key performance metrics such as heat transfer efficiency, pressure drop, system stability, and operational range, thereby optimizing fluid selection for specific engineering applications.

ideas.title: Symmetry-Informed Inverse Boundary Condition Estimation for Convective Flows
ideas.description: This research fuses Lie group symmetry analysis with inverse problem techniques to develop a novel framework for estimating unknown wall boundary conditions in fluid flow and heat transfer. The core idea is to apply Lie symmetry methods to the governing PDEs not just for solution reduction, but to systematically derive optimal similarity transformations that recast the inverse problem into an ordinary differential equation (ODE) framework in the similarity space. This transformation drastically reduces the complexity of the parameter estimation, as the unknown boundary condition becomes embedded in the scaling of the similarity variable itself. Consequently, downstream temperature profile data can be used to robustly infer the wall suction or stretching rate, even in cases where the boundary condition is coupled to the thermal field in a non-trivial way, a problem intractable for either approach alone.

ideas.title: Adapting Phonon Scattering Models for Microscale Slip and Jump Conditions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational no-slip assumption in fluid dynamics by introducing velocity slip and thermal jump conditions at solid boundaries, analogous to how nanoscale heat transfer challenges Fourier's law. The parallel problem is found in solid-state physics, where phonon transport at interfaces is modeled using the Boltzmann Transport Equation with boundary conditions defined by a specularity parameter. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing a generalized boundary condition for micro/nanofluidic flows where slip velocity and temperature jump are derived from a kinetic theory approach. The key adaptation is to define surface accommodation coefficients (for momentum and energy) that modulate the slip and jump lengths, making them dependent on surface morphology and intermolecular forces, thereby creating a more physically accurate model for flows in micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS), porous media, and superhydrophobic surfaces.

ideas.title: Transient Startup Dynamics with Anisotropic Slip: Unsteady Flow Development from Rest
ideas.description: This research numerically investigates the detailed, transient startup of a fluid flow from rest, specifically focusing on the initial unsteady phase before a steady state is achieved, but with the critical addition of anisotropic slip boundary conditions at the solid surface. The core novelty lies in analyzing how the differing slip coefficients for velocity components tangential and normal to the boundary sheet interact with and direct the evolving flow structures during this dynamic onset period. This combined approach reveals the coupled temporal and directional effects of slip, probing questions of how anisotropic surface properties can be used to control the development time, stability, and eventual steady-state configuration of a flow from its inception.

ideas.title: Multi-species reactive transport in a porous medium over a Riga plate
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the coupled flow, heat, mass transfer, and multi-species chemical kinetics within a porous medium saturated by an electrically conducting fluid over a Riga plate. The model extends beyond binary reactions to include networks of competing or sequential reactions among multiple chemical species. The synergy lies in analyzing how the porous matrix's tortuosity and surface area interact with the plate's electromagnetic damping to control the residence time, mixing, and spatial distribution of reactants, thereby dictating the overall yield and selectivity of complex chemical processes in this hybrid configuration.

ideas.title: Coupled experimental-theoretical investigation of hybrid nanofluid dynamics in a dynamically deforming microchannel
ideas.description: This research integrates precision micro-fabrication, advanced flow diagnostics, and multi-scale modeling to overcome the validation gap in hybrid nanofluid flow studies. An experimental module featuring a stretchable PDMS microchannel with embedded actuators enables real-time control of wall deformation, while micro-PIV and thermal imaging measure flow and heat transfer characteristics of a well-characterized hybrid nanofluid. These experimental results are used to rigorously calibrate and validate a concurrent computational model that resolves nanoparticle aggregation and interfacial phenomena. The outcome is a robust, experimentally grounded understanding of the coupled effects of wall stretching, nanoparticle synergy, and micro-confinement on convective transport.

ideas.title: Multiscale Hybrid Framework with First-Principles Slip/Jump Closure
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid simulation framework that dynamically determines slip and thermal jump boundary conditions from first principles, eliminating the need for empirical coefficients. A continuum Navier-Stokes solver is coupled to a localized molecular-scale patch at the fluid-solid interface, which uses MD or DSMC to compute the local momentum and energy accommodation. The system iteratively passes the local flow state to the molecular patch and receives back the computed effective slip length and temperature jump, enabling predictive simulations of microscale and nanoscale flows with realistic, complex surfaces where accommodation coefficients are unknown or variable.

ideas.title: Adaptive Hybrid Radiation Transport with Dynamic Model Selection
ideas.description: This research develops an adaptive framework for radiative heat transfer that overcomes the robustness and efficiency limitations of a globally applied non-linear model. The system intelligently selects the most appropriate radiation model—linear diffusion, non-linear diffusion, or a higher-fidelity transport method—for each computational cell based on local optical depth and temperature gradient thresholds. This hybrid approach maintains the accuracy of the non-linear approximation where it is most needed, such as in steep gradient regions or near interfaces, while defaulting to faster linear models in optically thick, smooth regions. The result is a robust, computationally tractable simulation tool that captures non-linear radiative effects without sacrificing stability or performance for large-scale engineering problems.

ideas.title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Physically Consistent Riga Plate Parameter Optimization
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to optimize Riga plate parameters for target flow and heat transfer profiles. The neural network is constrained during training to satisfy the fundamental governing partial differential equations (PDEs) for magnetohydrodynamic flow and heat transfer, embedding physical laws directly into the model. This ensures all optimized parameter sets adhere to conservation principles, drastically reduces the required volume of high-fidelity simulation data for training, and provides more reliable extrapolation. The resulting system functions as a hybrid data-physics optimizer, generating physically plausible designs while maintaining the efficiency of a machine learning surrogate.

ideas.title: Infer Wall Motion from Thermal Data via Geophysical Inverse Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from geophysical inverse problems, such as seismic tomography, to estimate wall boundary conditions (suction or stretching rate) from measured temperature profiles downstream. In geophysics, sparse surface data are used with regularization to infer unseen subsurface properties. The adapted idea formulates the thermal-fluid problem as an optimization: a forward solver simulates the flow and temperature field for a guessed boundary condition; the difference between its predicted temperatures and the measured profiles defines a cost function. This ill-posed inverse problem is stabilized using Tikhonov regularization, penalizing unphysical oscillations in the estimated wall motion, and solved iteratively to recover the boundary condition that best explains the thermal measurements.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Subsurface Mapping to Model Spatially-Variable Thermal Properties
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to incorporate variable thermal properties by drawing an analogy from geophysics and hydrogeology. In that field, the challenge of spatially variable hydraulic conductivity is solved using geostatistical interpolation (kriging) based on discrete borehole data and a spatial correlation model (variogram). Adapted to the thermal modeling context, the solution involves using discrete laboratory measurements of thermal conductivity and heat capacity from material samples as input data. A thermal property variogram would be developed to define spatial correlation lengths, and ordinary kriging would then be applied to generate a continuous, heterogeneous 2D or 3D field of thermal properties for the simulation domain, thereby significantly increasing the model's physical realism compared to using constant or simply layered values.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Flow Models for Industrial Coating Process Simulation
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Geophysical Fluid Dynamics to scale up industrial coating process simulation. The analogous problem—modeling turbulent flows over planetary-scale complex terrains—is solved using multi-scale hybrid models (e.g., LES-RANS coupling) to balance accuracy and computational cost. The adapted idea applies this principle by developing a hybrid simulation framework for high-speed, large-area film coating. It couples a high-fidelity model for the critical coating application zone (meniscus, wetting line) with a coarser, efficient model for the vast stretching sheet domain, enabling the prediction of coating uniformity and defects across industrial-scale processes while remaining computationally tractable.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Thermal Fields for Target Nanoparticle Patterning via Coupled Transport and Optimization
ideas.description: This research develops a computational framework that couples a forward model of nanoparticle transport (including aggregation, thermophoresis, and Brownian motion) with an inverse optimization routine. The system is designed to solve for the necessary experimental parameters—such as spatially varying temperature gradients and flow fields—to achieve a user-specified, non-uniform nanoparticle distribution. This transforms the model from a passive predictor into an active design tool. The output directly guides the setup of lab-on-a-chip or material synthesis experiments to fabricate structures with tailored optical, thermal, or mechanical properties.

ideas.title: Anisotropic Slip from Anisotropic Friction in Tribology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the standard assumption of isotropic slip at a boundary by proposing an anisotropic slip condition, inspired by the modeling of anisotropic friction in tribology. In tribology, surfaces with directional textures or molecular alignments exhibit different friction coefficients depending on the sliding direction, solved using directional decomposition or tensorial friction laws. Adapted to fluid dynamics, this means applying different slip coefficients (or slip lengths) for velocity components tangential and normal to a predefined direction on the sheet (e.g., along vs. across micro-grooves). The research would involve formulating a tensorial slip boundary condition, implementing it in a flow solver, and studying its impact on near-wall velocity profiles and overall flow resistance in anisotropic micro- or nanofluidic systems.

A dedicated experimental apparatus is designed to replicate key aspects of the 3D flow and heat transfer problem, providing detailed, spatially-resolved validation data. This data is then used within a formal calibration framework to inversely identify and update uncertain simulation inputs, such as turbulence constants or boundary heat losses. The final output is a rigorously validated and predictive digital model, rather than an untested numerical result.

ideas.title: Apply Lattice Boltzmann Method with Multi-Component Traffic Flow Analogy for Hybrid Nanofluid Simulation
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using the Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBM) to simulate hybrid nanofluid flow. By drawing an analogy from traffic flow modeling, where LBM successfully simulates interactions between different vehicle classes (cars, trucks), we can adapt that solution. In traffic engineering, multi-class LBM uses separate distribution functions and interaction rules for each vehicle type to capture complex mesoscopic behaviors. This approach is adapted to model hybrid nanofluids by treating the base fluid and each distinct nanoparticle type (e.g., Al2O3 and CuO with different shapes) as separate 'classes.' The adapted research idea develops a multi-component LBM framework where each nanoparticle class has tailored collision operators and forcing terms to capture shape-dependent transport, aggregation, and interaction with the fluid and other particles. This provides a fundamental mesoscopic tool to predict the effective thermophysical properties and flow dynamics of hybrid nanofluids, moving beyond homogeneous mixture assumptions.

ideas.title: Adapting Polymer Rheology's Time-Temperature Superposition to Nanofluid Property Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the constant property assumption by proposing a novel modeling framework adapted from polymer rheology. The analogous problem is the characterization of temperature-dependent viscoelastic properties in polymers, solved using the time-temperature superposition (TTS) principle to create predictive master curves. The adapted solution applies a similar superposition concept to the thermophysical properties of hybrid nanofluids. Experimental data for viscosity (across shear rates) and thermal conductivity, measured at several discrete temperatures, would be shifted to construct continuous master curves at a chosen reference temperature. The core innovation is developing a 'temperature-concentration superposition' shift factor equation that mathematically encapsulates how temperature and nanoparticle loading interact to alter the base fluid's properties, providing a more physically grounded and predictive model than empirical curve-fitting alone.

ideas.title: Analytical-Numerical Homotopy Framework for High-Fidelity Transient Startup Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction develops a hybrid analytical-numerical framework where the Homotopy Analysis Method (HAM) is specifically tailored to solve the governing nonlinear ODEs during the critical transient startup phase from rest. Instead of seeking a steady-state series solution, the HAM formulation will embed the initial time-like variable, producing a semi-analytical temporal solution series that captures the evolution from the initial condition. This analytical scaffold provides a high-fidelity reference to validate and diagnose the performance of detailed numerical simulations in the initial unsteady phase, where numerical errors and stiffness are most pronounced. The synergy lies in using the analytical series to inform and improve numerical schemes, while using numerical results to optimize HAM convergence parameters, creating a closed-loop methodology for unprecedented accuracy in startup flow analysis.

ideas.title: Stability Analysis of Dual Flow Solutions via Energy Landscape Methods from Statistical Physics
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of performing stability analysis on dual flow solutions by drawing an analogy from statistical physics and materials science. In those fields, the problem of determining which metastable phase (e.g., a specific crystal structure or glassy state) is physically realized from multiple possible states is solved by analyzing the system's energy landscape—calculating the depth of local minima and the activation barriers between them. We adapt this solution to fluid dynamics. The adapted idea is to conceptualize different steady flow solutions as distinct minima in a generalized energy or Lyapunov functional landscape. The research would involve developing or applying numerical methods (akin to nudged elastic band techniques) to map this landscape for a given flow system, quantifying the relative stability of each dual solution and the energy barriers separating them. This approach would predict transition probabilities between flow states under finite-amplitude perturbations and identify the physically dominant, realizable solution based on the landscape's topology and the system's initial conditions.

ideas.title: General tensorial slip boundary conditions for arbitrarily oriented anisotropic surfaces
ideas.description: This idea implements a full tensorial (or matrix) slip boundary condition to model surfaces with directional slip properties that are not aligned with the simulation geometry. The slip length becomes a second-rank tensor, defined in the local coordinate system of the anisotropic material, which is then rotated into the global frame for the flow solver. This approach correctly captures the coupling between velocity components when the material's high-slip and low-slip directions are oblique to the flow. It enables the study of complex, real-world scenarios like flow over misaligned graphene layers, textured surfaces, or composite materials where the slip anisotropy is an intrinsic material property with an arbitrary orientation.

ideas.title: Discovery of Non-Intuitive Riga Plate Topologies via Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Flow Control
ideas.description: This research develops a physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework to explore the vast, high-dimensional design space of Riga plate magnet and electrode arrangements. The PINN is trained not only on sparse simulation or experimental data but is fundamentally constrained by the underlying magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) and energy equations. This allows the system to propose and evaluate unconventional, topologically complex configurations that satisfy physical laws while optimizing for multi-objective targets like specific flow profiles or heat transfer enhancement. The outcome is a generative design tool that discovers novel, physically-realizable Riga plate topologies for advanced active flow control.
