ideas.description: This research moves beyond a comparative study to a goal-oriented design optimization of the Riga plate's electromagnetic layout. A specific flow control objective, such as maximizing near-wall mixing or achieving a uniform exit velocity, is first defined. A coupled numerical model simulates the magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) flow for any given arrangement of magnets and electrodes. An automated optimization framework then iteratively adjusts the spatial configuration and strengths of these elements to find the design that best fulfills the predefined objective, providing a principled method for designing application-specific MHD actuators.

ideas.title: Optimizing Riga Plate Flow Control via Biomimetic Electrode and Magnet Arrays
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea to explore different magnetic field configurations on a Riga plate by drawing an analogy from biomedical engineering, specifically the design of neural stimulation arrays. The analogous problem—focusing electrical stimulation on specific neural tissues using optimized multi-electrode geometries—is solved through computational field modeling and iterative array pattern design. Adapted to the Riga plate context, this involves studying the flow under different arrangements by designing and testing complex, non-uniform arrays of magnets and electrodes, inspired by patterns like concentric rings or fractal grids used in neural interfaces. Computational simulations (e.g., using finite element methods) will map the resulting Lorentz force distributions for each configuration, with the aim of discovering novel arrangements that provide superior, targeted control over boundary layer flow characteristics such as separation, stability, and heat transfer, moving beyond traditional uniform layouts.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Specific, Regime-Bounded Integration of Dominant Thermal Property Variation
ideas.description: This work moves beyond a generic suggestion by first conducting a local sensitivity analysis to identify the single most influential variable thermal property (e.g., conductivity, heat capacity) and its primary driver (temperature, phase change) within the model's operational envelope. A specific, physics-based constitutive relation (e.g., a polynomial or piecewise function derived from empirical data pre-2026) is then integrated, with its parameters explicitly defined and measurable. The analysis is rigorously bounded to the physical regime where this variation is significant, and the impact on key model outputs is quantified against the constant-property baseline to validate the necessity of the added complexity.

ideas.title: A Comparative Framework for Non-Newtonian Fluid Models Using a Rheological 'Phylogeny'
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to extend analysis beyond the Williamson fluid model by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's phylogenetic methods. The analogous solution involves constructing evolutionary trees to map relationships among diverse species. Adapted to non-Newtonian fluid dynamics, this means developing a systematic comparative framework—a 'rheological phylogeny'—that classifies fluid models (e.g., Casson, Herschel-Bulkley, Oldroyd-B, Carreau) based on their fundamental constitutive characteristics. This framework will organize the theoretical landscape, elucidate the relationships and assumptions connecting different models, and provide a predictive tool for understanding the potential behavior of complex or hybrid fluids based on their position in the classification structure.

ideas.description: This approach abandons the quest for a single analytical nonlinear Rosseland approximation. Instead, it employs a machine-learned model to predict a nonlinear radiation flux closure, where the model is explicitly conditioned on local material and thermodynamic state variables. To prevent error propagation and ensure physical fidelity, the system selectively calls high-fidelity discrete ordinates or implicit Monte Carlo solvers in computational cells identified as high-uncertainty or critical to the overall solution. This creates a self-correcting, adaptive method that maintains computational efficiency where possible while guaranteeing accuracy where needed.

ideas.title: Global Bifurcation Mapping and Stability Landscape of Flow Solutions
ideas.description: This research develops an integrated computational framework that first maps the complete set of possible steady flow solutions for a given configuration using global bifurcation analysis and numerical continuation methods. It then performs rigorous linear and nonlinear stability analysis on every discovered solution branch to classify their stability and basins of attraction. The approach actively searches for bifurcation points to uncover new solution families, ensuring no physically relevant (even unstable) states are missed. The final output is a comprehensive stability landscape that identifies all physically realizable solutions and explains transitions between them.

ideas.title: Generative Thermodynamic Optimization via Entropy Generation Minimization
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop, generative design framework that uses entropy generation minimization as the primary objective for thermodynamic optimization. The system integrates second-law analysis with multi-physics simulations and an automated optimization algorithm to actively explore the design space. It iteratively adjusts system parameters—such as geometry, operating conditions, and material properties—to converge on configurations that minimize irreversibilities. This approach transforms entropy analysis from a diagnostic tool into a predictive and generative engine for creating inherently efficient thermodynamic systems.

ideas.title: Lie Group-Informed Lattice Boltzmann Method for Hybrid Nanofluid Symmetry Discovery and Simulation
ideas.description: This research direction integrates Lie group symmetry analysis directly into the Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBM) framework for hybrid nanofluid flows. The Lie group analysis is applied to the continuous PDEs governing nanofluid transport to identify novel similarity transformations and invariant solutions. These analytical symmetry reductions are then used to inform and constrain the mesoscopic LBM simulation, for example, by designing specialized collision operators or deriving exact boundary conditions for the distribution functions. The synergy creates a physics-informed, multi-scale approach where analytical symmetries guide and accelerate the LBM, enabling efficient simulation of complex flows on reduced computational domains while preserving the detailed particle interaction physics that standard model reduction techniques would lose.

ideas.title: Analyze Transient Startup Using Phase Space Reconstruction from Neuroscience
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational neuroscience to study transient startup from rest. The analogous problem is analyzing the high-dimensional, unsteady neural population dynamics that precede a stable cognitive state, which is solved using phase space reconstruction and time-delay embedding to map the trajectory from rest to an attractor. The adapted idea applies this method to numerical analysis of initial transients in physical systems (e.g., fluid flow startup). By performing time-delay embedding on early-time numerical data of key variables, the research will reconstruct the low-dimensional phase space manifold of the transient, enabling detailed analysis of the scaling of initial instabilities, critical transitions, and the precise dynamical path taken before the system settles into its steady state.

ideas.title: Replace linear radiation with nonlinear radiation using a path-integral inspired closure
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the linearity assumption in the classic Rosseland approximation for thermal radiation at high temperatures, where radiation pressure and material property dependence on temperature and radiation intensity become significant. Drawing an analogy from Quantum Field Theory, specifically the use of the Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation to handle nonlinear interactions, we propose a novel solution. By formally treating the nonlinear radiation transport problem as a statistical field theory, we introduce an auxiliary field to decouple the nonlinearity in the photon absorption/emission process. The adapted research idea is to apply this field-theoretic decoupling technique to derive a new, nonlinear Rosseland-like approximation. The saddle-point solution for the auxiliary field provides a self-consistent, closed-form expression for the radiative heat flux as a nonlinear function of the temperature gradient and local radiation energy density, effectively replacing the linear constitutive law with a non-perturbative, nonlinear closure.

ideas.description: This research develops a high-fidelity computational model specifically designed to simulate the origin and evolution of critical defects in industrial film coating processes. The system couples transient, non-isothermal, viscoelastic flow with a moving mesh to capture dynamic instabilities such as draw resonance, neck-in, and edge-bead formation on a stretching sheet. By explicitly modeling these failure modes, the framework identifies stable operating windows and guides the design of die lip geometries and cooling strategies to mitigate defects. The outcome is a predictive virtual prototyping tool that directly targets yield improvement and robust scale-up from lab to production.

ideas.title: Robust Bayesian-Physics-Informed Estimation of Boundary Conditions from Noisy Thermal Data
ideas.description: This adversarially improved system tackles the ill-posedness of the original inverse problem by combining deep learning with physical constraints and uncertainty quantification. A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) is trained to solve the coupled flow and thermal fields, with the unknown boundary condition (suction/stretching rate) treated as a learnable parameter, ensuring all solutions obey the governing PDEs. A Bayesian inference layer, using variational inference or Markov Chain Monte Carlo, is then applied to the network's parameter space to produce a posterior distribution over the possible boundary conditions, given the noisy temperature measurements. This outputs not just a single estimate but a statistically robust range of plausible values with quantified confidence, making the method applicable to real experimental data where noise and model error are unavoidable.

ideas.title: Adapting Information-Theoretic Channel Capacity Analysis to Thermodynamic Entropy Generation Optimization
ideas.description: This research adapts the channel capacity optimization framework from information theory to perform second-law analysis and thermodynamic optimization. In communication systems, Shannon's theorem solves the analogous problem of maximizing information transfer through noisy channels by optimizing signal characteristics relative to noise entropy. Here, we treat a thermodynamic system as an information channel: the ideal reversible process represents the input signal, irreversibilities (friction, heat transfer across finite temperature differences, mixing) represent channel noise, and the actual work output represents the received signal. We will develop a formalism to calculate 'thermodynamic mutual information' between ideal and actual process paths, quantify entropy generation as the 'equivocation' or information loss, and optimize system design and operating parameters to maximize the useful work 'capacity' of the system. This provides a novel, unified optimization metric that directly links entropy generation minimization to the maximization of a system's ability to deliver useful work.

ideas.title: Modal analysis of impulsive-start transients to characterize intrinsic system dynamics
ideas.description: This research focuses on the transient phase following an impulsive change in a boundary condition (e.g., a step in pressure gradient, wall velocity, or inflow) from a well-defined, quiescent initial state. High-resolution numerical simulations capture the ensuing spatiotemporal evolution. The core analysis applies dynamic mode decomposition (DMD) to this dataset to identify dominant frequencies, growth/decay rates, and coherent spatial structures that characterize the system's intrinsic response. This approach moves beyond describing a single startup event to providing a generalized, modal framework that predicts transient behavior, links it to linear stability eigenmodes, and offers a basis for reduced-order modeling of the unsteady pathway to steady state.

ideas.title: Apply Homotopy Analysis Method with Convergence Control from Quantum Perturbation Theory
ideas.description: This research adapts convergence control strategies from quantum perturbation theory to enhance the Homotopy Analysis Method (HAM). In quantum mechanics, techniques like resummation and renormalization are used to tame divergent perturbation series and extend their utility. By analogy, this idea proposes to integrate an adaptive, optimization-based convergence control mechanism into HAM's framework. Instead of relying on a user-defined, constant convergence-control parameter, the method will employ an algorithm—inspired by resummation logic—to optimally select or adjust auxiliary parameters or basis functions at each homotopy order. This adaptation aims to systematically guarantee and accelerate the convergence of the analytical approximations for nonlinear governing equations, making HAM more robust and automated for problems with strong nonlinearities or boundary layers.

ideas.title: Multi-species reaction modeling using ecological competition frameworks
ideas.description: This research extends binary chemical reaction models to multiple reacting species by drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology's solution to multi-species competition. The Lotka-Volterra competition equations successfully model complex ecological communities through coupled differential equations with interaction coefficients between all species pairs. Adapted to chemical kinetics, this approach replaces discrete binary reaction modeling with a continuous interaction matrix framework where each species' concentration dynamics depend on coefficients representing reaction affinities with all other species. This enables prediction of complex multi-species chemical equilibria, oscillatory reaction networks, and emergent behaviors that cannot be captured by simple binary reaction models, fundamentally challenging the assumption that complex chemical systems can be adequately described as sums of pairwise interactions.
